Introduction
A floppy infant, painless foot drop, and a paralyzed child are poten-
tial manifestations of motor neuron disease (MND). Few diseases in 
medicine cause as much concern as presentations of spinal muscular 
atrophy (SMA), amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), and poliomyeli-
tis. Few diseases as rare as ALS are a household name, yet Lou  
Gehrig disease (synonymous with ALS or simply MND) is widely 
known even by those who never saw the “Iron Horse” play baseball 
or know the full story of the great man after whom the disease is 
named. MNDs include a heterogeneous group of disorders associ-
ated with dysfunction and degeneration of motor neurons, alone or 
in combination with other neuron pools. The selective and relentless 
degeneration or dysfunction of motor neurons, a common thread of 
most MNDs, invokes a remarkable yet rational degree of fear in 
patients and providers alike. This can only recently be mixed with 
hope, as new breakthroughs are changing the grim prognosis of 
some forms of MND. The objective of this chapter is to provide  
an overview of the spectrum of MNDs and outline the general  
approach to evaluation, diagnosis, and management of MNDs.
General Features of Motor Neuron Disease
Classification
MNDs can be broadly grouped into inherited or acquired causes. 
The main acquired or sporadic causes include poliomyelitis, ALS, 
and ALS variants. Rarer acquired forms include immune-  
mediated, endocrine, traumatic, nonpoliomyelitis infections and 
paraneoplastic etiologies. The most common inherited causes of 
MND include SMA, familial ALS, and X-linked bulbospinal 
muscular atrophy (Kennedy disease [KD]). MNDs can also be 
divided into groups based on selectivity for loss of upper motor 
neurons (UMNs; corticospinal and corticobulbar tracts) or lower 
motor neurons (LMNs; spinal anterior horn cells and cranial 
nerve LMNs) (Box 41.1).
Assessment
History
A thorough history and physical examination are vital when  
assessing a patient with a possible MND. Patients with MNDs 
usually present with painless weakness. The initial presentation is 
focal in Hirayama disease (HD) and can be in ALS and poliomy-
elitis. However, less prominent generalized weakness is also often 
present, but it is only found on a directed history. Associated 
features, such as prominent sensory symptoms or complaints  
attributable to sphincter or autonomic dysfunction, are unusual 
in pure motor neuron processes and should prompt consideration 
of an alternative process. The history should begin by focusing on 
when the patient (or family member) first noticed symptoms 
along with the distribution and characteristics of these symptoms. 
Establishing the rate of progression is helpful. A very slowly pro -
gressive course may suggest an inherited disorder. In this case, a 
disease-modifying treatments that target the genetic cause 
of the disease.
• Poliomyelitis, a viral-mediated MND, has been largely eradicated 
globally, though rare cases still occur because of wild-type and 
vaccine-derived strains as well as other infections, such as West 
Nile virus.
• Less common forms of MNDs include Hirayama disease, distal 
SMA, and paraneoplastic MND.
• ALS is universally fatal, yet various symptomatic and disease-
modifying treatments, along with rehabilitation measures, can 
significantly prolong and improve the quality of life for affected 
patients.
• Rehabilitation measures for MNDs are essential to enhancing 
function, extending survival, and improving the quality of life 
for patients with MNDs.
41
Motor Neuron Diseases
SHAWN P. JORGENSEN, ARSH KETABFOROUSH, AND W. DAVID ARNOLD
• Motor neuron diseases (MNDs) are a group of neuromuscular 
disorders that affect the anterior horn cells or the cortical motor 
neurons, or both.
• Clinical features of MNDs may include both upper motor neuron 
features (spasticity, increased tone, or brisk reflexes) and lower 
motor neuron features (weakness, atrophy, and fasciculations).
• An important characteristic of most MNDs is the absence of 
overt sensory symptoms such as pain, numbness, and tingling.
• MNDs can be inherited or acquired, manifesting in either child -
hood or adulthood.
• Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) is characterized by its 
unique involvement of both upper and lower motor neurons, 
resulting in distinctive clinical manifestations.
• Spinal muscular atrophy (SMA), once the leading  
genetic cause of infant mortality, now has three available

sion. Asking about difficulty with specific tasks is also important. 
A thorough family history is essential to assess for any clues of an 
underlying hereditary process, and sometimes examination of 
family members is necessary to identify similar but unrecognized 
clinical features. When interviewing a patient with suspected 
MND, it is recommended that a history covering at least three 
generations is obtained and that the presence of not only symp -
toms of MND but also frontotemporal dementia (FTD), other 
dementias, parkinsonism, psychiatric disease, and suicidal tenden-
cies is sought because they may be varied manifestations of the 
same mutation if ALS is suspected. 165 An autosomal dominant 
pattern is especially important because the majority of identified 
genetic causes of ALS are transmitted in this manner. 165
Respiratory or bulbar involvement can be subtle and may take 
some probing to identify; features such as orthopnea or morning 
headaches may suggest diaphragm paralysis or nocturnal hy -
poventilation, respectively. The past medical history and review  
of systems can identify any systemic processes that may be related 
to MND. The functional and social history will be critical in 
prescribing orthotics, assistive devices, and therapy as well as 
eventually modifying the patient’s living situation as the disease 
progresses. Physicians should expect to become familiar with  
family support and living conditions to anticipate transitional 
challenges with disease progression.
Physical Exam
A detailed physical examination can help distinguish between  
different MNDs and tailor their diagnostic and management ap -
proaches. Examination should detail the presence of muscle atro-
phy, weakness, fasciculations and alterations of muscle tone, 
reflexes, bulbar function, gait, and coordination. Muscle atrophy, 
weakness, and fasciculations are the primary features of LMN 
dysfunction that should be sought. Fasciculations may be obvious 
on general observation or may require prolonged inspection of 
various muscle groups. T ongue fasciculations are the most readily 
identified evidence of LMN injury in the bulbar muscles. Fascicu-
lations near the surface of the muscle are visible on exam, but 
fasciculations occurring deep within the muscle may only be seen 
with needle electromyography or with muscle ultrasound. 134 The 
implications of fasciculations depend almost entirely on the clini-
cal context. In isolation, fasciculations are not pathognomonic for 
MND and can be seen in healthy individuals as well as numerous 
other conditions of the peripheral nervous system. Fasciculations 
should be interpreted in the context of other concomitant clinical 
and electrodiagnostic findings of active and chronic denervation. 
Findings of fasciculations in a patient with weakness and evidence 
of denervation on electromyography (EMG) are strongly sugges -
tive of MND, but when fasciculations are found in isolation, they 
are nonspecific and may not be pathologic.
Laboratory Studies
The laboratory approach to the patient with possible MND 
should be guided by features on history and clinical examination 
and is generally used to help exclude alternate diagnoses. Creatine 
kinase (CK) levels are often modestly elevated in MND and may 
confuse providers when evaluating a patient with painless 
weakness. Muscle biopsies are sometimes ordered unnecessarily 
because of the confusion this creates. Muscle biopsy is not 
normally necessary in patients with suspected MND unless the 
presentation is atypical or myopathy is suspected. Neurofilament 
light chain (NFL) is a more recently available laboratory test that 
becomes elevated in the blood or cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) when 
axonal damage occurs in the central or peripheral nervous system. 
NFL elevation, similar to how elevated CK levels indicate muscle 
damage in myopathies, can be used to detect, identify, and 
roughly quantify the degree of axon loss. Studies have found that 
NFL levels in CSF and blood samples can differentiate ALS  
patients from other neurologic conditions and healthy 
individuals.196 The potential of NFL levels as markers for moni -
toring disease progression and their correlation with disease 
severity is still unclear. More research is needed to confirm these 
associations and establish their reliability.
Genetic testing can, in some cases, confirm the diagnosis of 
hereditary MNDs, and other testing may not be warranted. Such 
examples may include testing for homozygous deletion or muta -
tion of the SMN1 gene for proximal SMA or testing for cytosine-
adenine-guanine (CAG) trinucleotide repeat number for possible 
KD. As genetic testing technologies and our understanding of the 
genetic causes of MND have improved, there is less reliance on an 
a priori approach to testing. Instead, multigene panels or exome 
or genome sequencing are now commonly used.
Imaging
Imaging studies may not be required in the evaluation of patients 
with suspected MND but are useful and sometimes of vital im -
portance to help exclude mimicking or confounding diagnoses 
causing UMN or LMN involvement.
Electrodiagnosis
Electrodiagnostic testing is the primary diagnostic modality to 
confirm loss or dysfunction of the peripheral nervous system. 
Electrodiagnostic studies in patients with suspected MND are 
designed to identify LMN involvement and to exclude other

mimicking disorders of the peripheral nervous system. Needle 
EMG is the most pertinent aspect of the electrodiagnostic exami-
nation for identification of LMN loss, but nerve conduction  
studies (NCS) are important to help exclude other diagnostic pos-
sibilities within the peripheral nervous system. Usually, needle 
EMG features of LMN involvement, such as fibrillation poten -
tials, fasciculation potentials, or neurogenic changes in motor unit 
action potential (MUAP) characteristics, are more readily appar -
ent than clinical features of LMN involvement. Motor NCS may 
remain normal until there is sufficient motor axonal loss to result 
in diminished compound muscle action potential amplitudes. 27 
In general, parameters of action potential propagation, including 
distal latencies and conduction velocities, will be relatively nor -
mal. Incidental findings of an asymptomatic or minimally symp -
tomatic neuropathy with symptoms occurring over a different 
time frame should not distract from the overall pattern of a sig -
nificant, primarily motor process. Some MNDs are associated 
with characteristic concomitant sensory involvement, although 
this is less severe than the findings of motor axonal loss. One 
important example is KD, which is usually associated with  
non–length-dependent sensory amplitude loss consistent with a 
coexistent sensory ganglionopathy. Other parameters of nerve 
conduction, including the latencies of F-waves and H-reflexes, are 
relatively preserved in proportion to the motor axonal loss.
Multifocal motor neuropathy (MMN) is one particular entity 
that deserves attention during the electrodiagnostic evaluation 
because it can closely mimic MND. MMN is an autoimmune 
disorder of the peripheral nerve associated with multifocal con -
duction block. Focal areas of conduction block in motor studies 
(but not sensory studies) outside of areas prone to entrapment can 
be seen in MMN. The motor abnormalities in MMN are typically 
more prominent in the upper limbs vs. the lower limbs and in 
distal limb muscles vs. proximal limb muscles. Multiple motor 
studies with multiple stimulation sites may be needed in some 
patients with suspected ALS to exclude subtle areas of conduction 
block that can be seen in MMN. Importantly, some patients with 
otherwise typical MMN will not have features of conduction 
block but still have favorable response to immunomodulatory 
treatment. Another distinctive feature that is typically evident on 
electrodiagnostic assessment of MMN is neurogenic involvement 
in the distribution of peripheral nerves rather than in the myoto -
mal distribution that is otherwise typical of MNDs.
Neuromuscular junction (NMJ) disorders also result in clinical 
phenotypes of pure motor deficits and should be high in the dif -
ferential diagnosis of a patient with suspected MND. Clinical 
features of prominent ocular, bulbar, and proximal limb weakness 
with fatigability are characteristic of a typical primary NMJ trans-
mission defect. Myasthenia gravis can most closely mimic MND 
with bulbar and LMN predominant involvement, such as pro -
gressive bulbar palsy (PBP). Electrodiagnostic studies to identify 
failure of NMJ transmission, including repetitive nerve stimula -
tion (RNS) or single-fiber EMG (SFEMG), should be considered, 
particularly in cases with evidence of fatigability on examination. 
Unfortunately, findings of NMJ transmission failure are not spe -
cific to primary NMJ disorders. Abnormal decrement may be seen 
on RNS studies in 20% of patients with ALS. 88 Similarly, 
SFEMG abnormalities of jitter and blocking can be seen in 
muscles that are normal on standard needle EMG in patients with 
MNDs, but such findings are usually associated with increased 
fiber density. The RNS and SFEMG abnormalities seen in MND 
are likely related to the inability of failing motor neurons to main-
tain sufficient synaptic output and the reduced safety factor at 
newly formed, immature synapses after sprouting and collateral 
reinnervation. This can make distinguishing primary NMJ disor-
ders from MND more difficult. However, other findings on nee -
dle EMG can further distinguish the two disease entities; for  
instance, neurogenic changes in MUAP size or recruitment are 
present in MND but absent in NMJ disorders. Fibrillations are 
typically present in MND but can also occur in some NMJ disor-
ders, including botulism, and rarely other disorders.
Other less common but helpful electrodiagnostic techniques 
are sometimes utilized in the diagnosis of MND for some pa -
tients. Macro EMG and motor unit number estimation (MUNE) 
are specialized techniques that can investigate motor unit func -
tion. Although usually utilized in the research setting, MUNE has 
shown promise as an outcome measure in preclinical and clinical 
trials of different types of MND, including SMA, ALS, and KD.
Motor Neuron Disease Treatment
General
T reatment of MND varies related to factors such as type of 
MND, disease duration, and severity. The majority of MNDs lack 
effective disease-modifying medical interventions (e.g. medica -
tions) to halt or counteract disease progression. Accordingly, 
multidisciplinary supportive care is essential to address disease 
impact and improve or maintain function. T reatments specific for 
individual MND will be discussed later with those individual 
disorders, as well as rehabilitation approaches and symptomatic 
and medical treatment for MNDs in general. Box 41.2 summa-
rizes ALS and SMA treatments.
Disease-Modifying Medications
The majority of MNDs have no or limited treatment, and his -
torically there have been no treatments available with a large dis -
ease-modifying effect for MNDs. Yet, at an ever-increasing rate, 
newer therapies are being developed for MNDs. Currently, there 
are multiple disease-modifying therapies for both ALS and SMA. 
Timely implementation of treatments to prevent (or slow) motor 
neuron loss is essential, and treatment should begin without delay.
Specific Diseases
ALS and ALS Variants
Sporadic and Familial ALS
ALS has an incidence of 1.4/100,000 and typically begins in the 
sixth to seventh decade of life. 144 Males are affected somewhat 
more than females, with a ratio of 1.6:1. 137 An important and 
distinctive feature of ALS is the degeneration of both the UMNs 
and LMNs, with relative sparing of other neurons. Although the 
understanding of ALS is ever expanding, the pathogenic mecha -
nisms remain incompletely defined. There are hereditary (familial) 
forms (fALS) and sporadic forms (sALS) of ALS, and these are 
indistinguishable on clinical presentation and evaluation.
Roughly 10% to 20% of patients with apparent sALS due to 
lacking family history are found to have a known genetic muta -
tion associated with fALS. 19 Superoxide dismutase 1 (SOD1) 
mutations were the first to be identified as a causative etiology of 
fALS.21 Mutations in SOD1 represent approximately 10% of 
fALS and are seen in approximately 1% of sALS. Even within the 
spectrum of SOD1-related ALS, there is considerable phenotypic 
variability. For instance, the D90A mutation of SOD1 portends 
an indolent course with ventilatory failure often occurring 10 years

SECTION 4  Issues in Specific Diagnoses
Key Recommendations in Specific Motor Neuron Diseases
Summary of Key Recommendations in the Treatment  
of Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis
Exercise
• Some form of therapeutic exercise should be offered to every patient with 
amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS)
• Range of motion and stretching exercises should be offered to every patient
• A gentle aerobic exercise program should be offered to ambulatory patients
• A resistance exercise program of mild-moderate resistance exercise for  
15 minutes should be offered
• A resistance exercise program of mild-moderate resistance exercise for  
15 minutes twice daily should be offered to all high-functioning patients
• Patients should avoid:
• Exercising to fatigue
• Exercising to dyspnea
• Eccentric exercise
• Patients should scale back their program if they have:
• Muscle cramping
• Excessive overuse weakness afterward
Nutrition
• Monitor weight every 3 months
• Percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) or radiologically inserted  
gastrostomy (RIG) tube
• Indications:
• Weight loss .10% of body weight
• Dehydration
• Meals limited by dysphagia and choking
• Not aspiration pneumonia
• Ensure all patients have accurate information regarding risks and survival 
advantages of PEG or RIG
• Refer willing patients for PEG or RIG before forced vital capacity (FVC) drops 
to ,50% predicted
• Refer willing patients with FVC between 50% and 30% for RIG
Medications
Riluzole
• Should be offered to every patient with ALS
• Dosage is 50 mg twice daily
• Treatment should be initiated as soon as possible
• Physicians should ensure patients have accurate information regarding riluzole
• Patients on riluzole should have liver function tests monitored
Edaravone
• Has been shown in one trial to help a select group of patients with ALS  
(Al-chalabi)5a,149a
• Was shown to be of no significant benefit to a less specific group of  
patients with ALS (Al-chalabi)
• Is generally well tolerated, with no known drug-drug interactions (Oskarrson 2018)
• Has not been shown to improve quality of life or survival (Al-chalabi)
• Is very expensive and requires frequent infusions
Tofersen
• For patients with SOD1-ALS
• Montly intrathecal injections
• Alternative Medications
• Patients should be advised that no other medication, nutritional supplement, 
or alternative medication has been shown to help patients with ALS
Multidisciplinary Clinics
• Extend survival and should be pursued when feasible
Mood Disorders
• Are common and should be treated to improve quality of life
Spasticity
• Intrathecal baclofen has been shown to work, but oral baclofen has not
• Botulinum toxin can be used but can lead to generalized weakness
Respiratory
• Respiratory measurements (usually FVC and maximal inspiratory pressure 
[MIP]) should be measured at every visit regardless of symptoms
• BOX 41.2
• Noninvasive ventilation (NIV)
• Extends survival, slows respiratory decline, and improves quality of life
• Commonly begun when FVC ,50% predicted and MIP #60 cm H2O or 
a PaCO2 of $45 mm Hg, although early NIV prolongs life
• Supplemental O2 suppresses respiratory drive and can worsen symptoms 
and lead to respiratory arrest and should only be used for palliation of 
symptomatic hypoxia
• A mechanical insufflator-exsufflator improves secretion clearance and can 
be used in patients with a peak cough flow ,300 to 350 L/min
Communication
• Is a major determinant of quality of life and should be optimized
• Patients should be routinely monitored by a speech-language pathologist
Palliative Care
• Discuss the patient’s wishes and involve a palliative care team as early as 
possible
• Patient’s distressing symptoms are often undertreated, and benzodiaze -
pines and opioids titrated to clinical effect will almost never cause  
life-threatening respiratory depression
Summary of Key Recommendations in the Treatment of SMA
Exercise
• Tailored physical therapy programs should be provided to maintain mobility 
and prevent joint contractures
• Range of motion exercises and stretching should be included
• Moderate aerobic activities should be encouraged as tolerated
Nutrition
• Regular nutritional assessments are crucial to manage feeding difficulties 
and ensure adequate caloric intake
• Gastrostomy tube feeding should be considered for patients with severe 
swallowing difficulties or significant weight loss
• Consult a dietitian for individualized dietary plans
Medications
Nusinersen
• Approved for all types of SMA
• Administered via intrathecal injection
• Shown to improve motor function and survival
Onasemnogene abeparvovec
• Approved for patients age ,2 years
• A single-dose intravenous gene therapy
• Aims to replace the defective SMN1 gene
Risdiplam
• Approved for patients age $2 months
• Oral medication that increases SMN protein production
Multidisciplinary Clinics
• Essential for comprehensive care, including physical medicine and rehabili -
tation, neurology, pulmonology, orthopedics, nutrition, and physical therapy
Respiratory Care
• Regular monitoring of respiratory function
• NIV for patients with respiratory insufficiency
• Airway clearance techniques and cough-assist devices to manage secretions
Orthopedic Care
• Regular assessments to monitor and manage scoliosis and joint contractures
• Surgical interventions may be necessary for severe scoliosis
Genetic Counseling
• Should be offered to families to discuss inheritance patterns, family  
planning, and genetic testing options
Palliative Care
• Engage palliative care early to address quality of life and symptom  
management
• Focus on holistic care, including emotional and psychological support  
for patients and families

after onset, whereas the A4V mutation is typically associated with 
rapid decline and death within 1 year of onset.22,23 Since the iden-
tification of SOD1 mutations over 20 years ago, numerous other 
ALS-related genes have been identified. Mutations in C9ORF72 
are the most frequently identified causative mutation in fALS  
and sALS, and they are responsible for approximately 40%  
of fALS and approximately 5% of sALS. 25–27 There are  
currently over 30 gene mutations associated with the clinical  
syndrome of fALS, and because of rapid advances in genetic  
technology, the molecular understanding of sALS and fALS is 
constantly expanding.
As more genes responsible for fALS and sALS are identified, 
our understanding of the disease’s underlying pathophysiology 
will improve. This advancement will enhance our ability to diag-
nose ALS, provide accurate prognoses, and design future therapies 
for patients. Clinically, fALS is similar to sALS but may be char-
acterized by a younger average age of onset.
29 It may progress 
faster or slower than sALS depending on the pathogenic muta -
tion.30 Furthermore, targeting the genetic cause of ALS offers 
treatment strategies.
ALS Variants
ALS involves both the UMNs and LMNs, but some variant forms 
of ALS may be restricted to only UMNs or LMNs or certain body 
regions. Such variants include primary lateral sclerosis (PLS) and 
progressive muscular atrophy (PMA), where neuron loss is re -
stricted to the UMNs or LMNs, respectively (Fig. 41.1). Similarly, 
there are regional variants, including PBP affecting the cranial 
nerve nuclei of the bulbar musculature, brachial amyotrophic 
diplegia (BAD) affecting the upper limbs in a proximal predominant 
manner, and leg amyotrophic diplegia (LAD) predominantly  
affecting the distal lower limbs.
56,103 Restriction to certain motor 
neuron pools or regions is often associated with a different prog -
nosis. Importantly, in many if not most cases, there is subsequent 
progression to generalized involvement of both UMNs and LMNs 
typical of classic ALS. The relationship between ALS and other 
variants is not entirely clear, but there is strong evidence that these 
disorders are, at a minimum, tightly related. Phenotypic variability 
in genetically defined cases of fALS related to single mutations 
suggests that variants of ALS may be merely different faces of the 
same underlying pathology.
Although the pathologic relationship between ALS and ALS 
variants remains uncertain, distinguishing between these variants 
is helpful for clinicians. All restricted variants, except for PBP , 
have a better prognosis than typical ALS. Patients with PLS, 
PMA, and PBP may not be candidates for clinical trials for emerg-
ing ALS treatments. Thus, despite their similarities, it is useful for 
clinicians and patients to cautiously use these diagnostic catego -
ries when applicable. Generally, the clinical evaluation and treat -
ment of patients with ALS and its variants are similar.
Primary lateral sclerosis.  PLS is a disorder associated with 
progressive spasticity and weakness of limb and bulbar muscles 
related to degeneration of UMNs. PLS is rare and typically seen in 
the fifth decade. Similar to other sporadic MNDs, the etiology is 
not known. PLS is defined as having little or no LMN involve -
ment clinically or by EMG. In spite of this, most patients with 
PLS eventually have fasciculations and cramps, some evidence of 
LMN loss on examination or EMG, and pathologic evidence of 
LMN involvement.
112 Although there are clear similarities with 
ALS, PLS progresses much more slowly than ALS—studies show 
an average lifespan of between 8 and 15 years after diagnosis.

SECTION 4  Issues in Specific Diagnoses
Although it may not be entirely clear that ALS and PLS are differ-
ent diseases, given the distinct clinical features and prognosis, it is 
as Charcot wrote: “the clinical description deserves to exist 
alone.”
37
Most patients with PLS initially present with unilateral leg 
spasticity that involves the other leg within approximately 1 to  
2 years. Signs and symptoms progress to the upper limbs within 
the next 3 to 4 years, and eventually there is pseudobulbar involve-
ment over the next 1 to 2 years.
200 Mills syndrome, a hemiplegic 
variant of PLS originally described in 1900, is associated with a 
unilateral presentation of UMN signs in the upper and lower 
limbs.
132 The primary findings on examination and the cause of 
disability in PLS are spasticity and clumsiness rather than overt 
weakness, consistent with an UMN syndrome.
112,200 Eventually, 
bulbar dysfunction is seen in nearly all patients. Unlike ALS, 
symptoms of bladder dysfunction are common late in PLS.
112,200
The diagnosis of PLS is established clinically, but testing can be 
helpful to exclude other diagnoses. Evaluation with magnetic 
resonance imaging (MRI) should be performed primarily to ex -
clude other central nervous system disorders, such as multiple 
sclerosis; however, cortical atrophy and increased T2 signal inten-
sity of the pyramidal tracts are often seen in PLS.
112,160 Severe 
abnormalities or absent potentials on transcranial magnetic stim-
ulation (TMS) are seen in nearly all cases of PLS and may confirm 
the diagnosis, but TMS is not widely available.
109,112,160 By defini-
tion, electrodiagnostic studies should not show significant LMN 
loss, but minor changes can be seen. The differential diagnosis for 
PLS includes other central nervous system disorders; ALS and 
hereditary spastic paraparesis are two such important conditions. 
The use of genetic analysis should be considered for hereditary 
spastic paraparesis, particularly in patients with relatively sym -
metric lower limb involvement. Electrodiagnostic studies should 
be performed to exclude LMN involvement, which if found may 
support or indicate ALS.
Progressive muscular atrophy. PMA is a MND marked by 
LMN degeneration, causing progressive weakness and muscular 
atrophy. PMA is rare and affects primarily older males. By defini-
tion, patients with PMA have no clinical evidence of UMN dys -
function at onset, but it is important to note that as many as 70% 
of patients with PMA will eventually demonstrate signs of UMN 
degeneration.
184 T echniques such as TMS and MRI spectroscopy 
can show subtle corticospinal tract involvement in over 50% of 
patients with PMA. In one autopsy series, nearly every patient 
showed ubiquitinated inclusions in LMNs, which is a pathogno-
monic sign of ALS.
93 It remains controversial whether PMA  
represents a disease distinct from ALS. Nevertheless, the general 
approach to PMA is similar to ALS, but because of the different 
presentation, treatment, and prognosis, it should be considered as 
a related but possibly distinct disorder. Patients with PMA show 
atrophy and weakness, beginning in the hands in nearly 50% of 
cases, and less commonly in the lower limbs, shoulder girdle, and 
bulbar musculature.
39 Fasciculations are usually evident on ex -
amination. Reflexes are reduced or absent, and features of UMN 
dysfunction are absent.
The approach to diagnosis and treatment of PMA is similar to 
that of ALS (both described in detail later). EMG abnormalities 
consist of fibrillation potentials, positive sharp waves, fascicula -
tion potentials, large MUAPs, and reduced MUAP recruitment. 
The differential diagnosis includes LMN-predominant ALS, other 
MNDs, and MMN. MMN is particularly important to exclude 
because this disorder is treatable with immunomodulation, such 
as immunoglobulin therapy. Prognosis is better than ALS, with a 
5-year survival rate of over 50%, and patients live an average of  
1 year longer than patients with ALS.
39,107 Older age, lower forced 
vital capacity (FVC), lower ALS Functional Rating Scale (ALSFRS), 
and widespread involvement are poor prognosticators.
39,107
Regional ALS variants.  The typical course of ALS involves  
focal onset and subsequent regional spread over time. Impor -
tantly, it is common for patients to have unrecognized mild or 
even moderate motor deficits in other regions, and at presentation 
the disease may be much more widespread on examination than 
recognized by the patient. During the natural course of ALS, most 
patients will develop generalized disease, but certain variants of 
ALS may remain restricted to certain regions or populations of 
motor neurons. Some of these variant syndromes include PBP , 
BAD, and LAD. PBP is an ALS variant with exclusively bulbar 
symptoms.
144 As mentioned, some patients may develop signifi-
cant limb involvement and eventually may be characterized as 
bulbar-onset ALS, but this is a less common transition (7.5%) 
than in PLS or PMA.
88 PBP comprises almost 10% of all MNDs, 
primarily affecting females. PBP has a significantly older average 
age of onset and carries a poorer prognosis compared with typical 
ALS.
33 BAD is a LMN syndrome that appears to be closely related 
to ALS.103 Patients with BAD typically have prominent proximal 
upper limb weakness that begins unilaterally and spreads to the 
contralateral limb. Distal upper limb muscles are relatively spared, 
and usually finger extensor muscles are more severely affected 
than finger flexors. Muscles of the bulbar, thoracic, and lumbar 
regions may remain unaffected for a decade or more, although in 
some cases there is more rapid progression. LAD is a lower limb 
correlate of BAD, but weakness tends to start in an asymmetric, 
distal-predominant manner with subsequent spread to the contra-
lateral lower limb. Similar to BAD, LAD may have delayed progres-
sion to other spinal regions and therefore a better prognosis. Inter-
estingly, patients with known causative ALS-related mutations may 
present with these variant syndromes, further supporting a close 
relationship between ALS and ALS variants.
187
ALS-plus syndromes. Although ALS is usually associated with 
isolated motor deficits, involvement outside of the motor system 
can occur. By definition, ALS-plus syndromes meet clinical and 
electrodiagnostic criteria for ALS, but they also have associated 
nonmotor neuron features that may include parkinsonism, FTD, 
ocular motility abnormalities, extrapyramidal signs, autonomic 
dysfunction, or sensory loss.
15,169 Certain forms of fALS have 
been associated with the development of nonmotor features. For 
instance, patients with the TAR DNA-binding protein (TAR-
DBP) gene mutations, known to cause fALS, have been reported 
with clinical features of parkinsonism.
69 Both FTD and fALS are 
seen in isolation and combination in patients and families with 
C9ORF72 gene mutations.
51,163 The association of nonmotor 
features in some patients with sporadic and fALS strongly suggests 
that ALS is a multisystem disease with predilection for UMN and 
LMN involvement.
ALS Assessment
Clinical presentation. Clinical presentations of ALS can vary 
greatly, but almost always include painless weakness and atrophy. 
The initial location of the weakness divides roughly into thirds: 
one-third in the bulbar region, one-third in the upper limb, and 
one-third in the lower limb.
33 In the limbs, weakness usually starts 
in an asymmetric and distally predominant fashion. 88 The weak-
ness seen in ALS often begins focally and then generalizes over 
time, though subtle weakness may be present in other areas the 
patient is not aware of. For instance, symptoms of foot drop or

hand weakness may prompt a clinical visit, and then more gener-
alized weakness, fasciculations, or hyperreflexia are noticed on 
exam. Patients may not clearly articulate complaints of weakness, 
but rather may describe difficulties with certain activities, such as 
climbing stairs, walking, or opening a jar. Sensation is not af -
fected, and the presentation of weakness with no numbness and 
tingling is a relatively rare clinical scenario that should raise red 
flags that MND or another pure motor process is present.
Failure of motor neurons leads to many clinical features outside 
of the limbs. Bulbar involvement in ALS at presentation is twice as 
common in females than in males and more common in older 
patients.40,88 Although around one-third of patients have bulbar 
symptoms as a chief complaint, the majority have such symptoms 
at presentation when specifically asked. 88 Those presenting with 
bulbar symptoms nearly always have dysarthria. 88 Dysphagia is a 
rare presenting complaint but is expected during the course of 
disease. Nearly one-quarter of patients acknowledge respiratory 
symptoms when specifically addressed.88 These may include sleep 
apnea, exertional dyspnea, or orthopnea. Isolated respiratory insuf-
ficiency at presentation is exceedingly rare; when respiratory 
involvement is noted at presentation, there are usually other gener-
alized deficits that are also apparent on examination.33,40 Presenting 
with isolated complaints of fasciculations is similarly rare, but it is 
not rare for fasciculations to be noted during the initial exam. 83 
Pain and other sensory symptoms are rare at the time of diagnosis 
and suggest an alternative diagnosis. An exception is cramping, 
which is the presenting complaint in around 10% of patients.
Spasticity, clumsiness, increased muscle stretch reflexes, and 
the Hoffmann sign are evidence of UMN degeneration. The 
Hoffmann sign in the upper limb and Babinski sign in the lower 
limb are useful signs of UMN pathology. In one series, 94% of 
patients with ALS and a positive Hoffmann sign had corticospinal 
tract degeneration on autopsy.195 The Babinski sign is less reliable, 
with only one-quarter of patients with ALS having a positive 
response bilaterally on presentation.83 Importantly, a normal reflex 
in an atrophic limb may represent an exaggerated reflex response. 
An exaggerated jaw jerk, pseudobulbar affect, positive snout, or 
palmomental reflexes can help establish UMN dysfunction in 
bulbar musculature. In ALS, there is typically a combination of 
UMN and LMN signs, starting asymmetrically and progressing 
to involve multiple regions. Increased reflexes are seen on presen-
tation in approximately one-half of patients with ALS. 83
Laboratory tests for ALS.  Laboratory testing is important in 
the diagnosis of ALS to assess for potential mimics, but no test 
currently available is specific to diagnose ALS. Importantly, some 
laboratory testing may show nonspecific abnormalities in patients 
with ALS, which can cause confusion. For instance, CK is  
often elevated, but typically less than 10 times the upper limit of 
normal.28 CSF protein may also be elevated, but rarely over  
60 mg/dL.88 Anti-GM1 antibodies are seen in MMN, an impor -
tant disorder in the differential of focal/multifocal weakness with-
out sensory loss or pain. The presence of these antibodies can help 
support the diagnosis of MMN but has been shown to be positive 
in up to 10% of patients with ALS. 88 This can pose a problem in 
patients without UMN signs, in whom MMN is an important 
consideration. In patients suspected of having ALS, genetic test -
ing can be helpful to determine the presence of a pathogenic 
mutation attributable to fALS. Outside of genetic testing in fALS, 
there are no laboratory tests that can establish the diagnosis of 
ALS. Similarly, in a patient whose clinical and electrophysiologic 
profile is strongly suggestive of ALS, there is no laboratory study 
that can effectively rule it out.28
Electrodiagnostics in ALS.  Electrodiagnosis is the main test 
used to diagnose ALS and should be done in nearly all cases where 
ALS is suspected. Abnormalities on needle EMG have the same 
significance as clinical evidence of LMN disease in the Gold Coast 
diagnostic criteria (see later). A combination of signs of active 
denervation (fibrillation potentials, positive sharp waves, and fas -
ciculations) and chronic denervation (high amplitude and wide 
duration MUAPs) are seen in most patients. Unstable MUAPs on 
triggered analysis are evidence of chronic denervation and imma -
ture synapse formation. Reduced recruitment is expected. The 
distribution of these findings correlates with the clinical deficits 
and usually progresses over time. Therefore, targeting clinically 
involved muscles increases the yield of needle EMG. Needle 
EMG is particularly useful to show subtle evidence of LMN dis -
ease in areas where it is not clinically evident. T wo muscles of 
different root and peripheral nerve supply must be involved to 
demonstrate LMN degeneration in a given region. 27 Early in  
the course of disease, there is focal LMN degeneration, and the 
majority of muscles on clinical and EMG assessments may be 
normal, making the diagnosis of ALS more challenging or impos-
sible. Thus, a high index of suspicion is needed to identify the 
disease earlier in the course. Motor NCS will show low amplitudes 
where significant axon loss has occurred but should otherwise be 
relatively normal. Evidence of conduction block suggests alternate, 
more treatable diagnoses such as MMN. Sensory conduction stud-
ies are usually normal, but coexistent sensory abnormalities can 
occur in ALS in up to 12% of patients, in which case findings are 
usually very mild compared with motor involvement.88
Imaging in ALS.  Imaging is frequently obtained in patients 
with suspected ALS. Corticospinal tract abnormalities can be seen 
on brain MRI but are nonspecific and not usually helpful diag -
nostically. MRI of the cervical spine can help to rule out structural 
lesions associated with cord compression or syringomyelia. It is 
worth noting that incidental findings of mild to moderate cervical 
spondylosis on imaging are not infrequent, and features of ALS 
should not be mistakenly attributed to clinically insignificant  
focal structural lesions. Furthermore, lack of sensory symptoms to 
corroborate myelopathy or radiculopathy may favor ALS. MRI of 
the brain is also commonly performed, particularly in a patient 
with bulbar or pseudobulbar symptoms or signs. Similar to cervi-
cal spine and incidental degenerative changes, approximately 10% 
of patients ultimately diagnosed with ALS will have incidental 
findings of nonspecific ischemic changes on a brain MRI. 88 In 
circumstances of focal cervical spine disease and mild ischemic 
changes identified on MRI, features of LMN loss (outside of  
the cervical region in the case of cervical spine disease) support  
the diagnosis of ALS as opposed to another process affecting the 
central nervous system.
ALS diagnostic criteria.  Diagnostic criteria have been devel -
oped to assist clinicians and researchers in identifying patients 
with ALS. T o overcome the limitations of previous categories, a 
consensus group of international ALS experts developed the Gold 
Coast criteria, which are simpler and more reflective of clinical 
practice ( Box 41.3 ). The key components of the Gold Coast  
criteria comprise (1) progressive motor impairment confirmed by 
either history or repeated clinical assessments, after an initial pe -
riod of normal motor function; (2) the presence of both UMN 
and LMN dysfunction in at least one body region (with both 
UMN and LMN dysfunction in the same region if only one 
region is affected) or LMN dysfunction in at least two body 
regions; and (3) the exclusion of diseases that mimic ALS through 
appropriate investigations.171

In a recent cohort study, the Gold Coast criteria showed sig -
nificantly higher sensitivity than both the prior Awaji and revised 
El Escorial definite/probable ALS criteria, underscoring their 
practical value.
172 Although the Gold Coast criteria showed a 
high level of sensitivity, highlighting the effectiveness of clinical 
assessment in identifying UMN dysfunction, there is room for 
improvement in ALS diagnosis through objective assessment of 
UMN dysfunction, an aspect that merits exploration in future 
prospective studies. The essence of the criteria is that discovering 
both UMN and LMN findings in the same area is rare and suspi-
cious for ALS. The more areas where these findings are identified, 
the more likely the diagnosis is ALS, and the less likely these 
findings can be realistically explained by any other disorder.
General approach to diagnosis of ALS. The overall approach 
to a patient with suspected ALS is complex and begins by estab -
lishing which type of motor neurons and which body regions are 
involved. A pragmatic approach is to first assess if there is clinical 
bulbar or pseudobulbar involvement. If so, an MRI of the brain 
should be obtained. If cervical, thoracic, and lumbar regions are 
involved, spinal MRI should be pursued or at least considered. 
This can help exclude the most common alternate diagnoses that 
are usually evident on neuroimaging, but the interpretation of 
minor, common MRI abnormalities needs to be done with cau -
tion. The differential can then be narrowed by the type of motor 
neurons that are clinically involved. Almost all patients should 
have an EMG performed. This helps determine the pattern of 
LMN involvement more accurately and makes the diagnostician 
more confident about the group of diagnoses to pursue.
The possible causes of both UMN and LMN injury in the 
same patient is a short list of rare conditions. A relatively common 
exception is cervical radiculomyelopathy related to degenerative 
cervical spine disease. A patient with this condition will have 
LMN injury and findings at the level of the radiculopathy, and 
UMN signs caudal to this level attributable to the myelopathy. A 
representative patient could have C6 cord compression from 
spondylosis. The resultant radiculopathy could lead to weakness 
and atrophy in the C6 myotome and diminished biceps and bra -
chioradialis reflexes. The myelopathy would cause an increase in 
the triceps and lower limb reflexes, Hoffmann and Babinski signs, 
and lower limb weakness or clumsiness without atrophy. This is a 
reasonably common and treatable finding in clinical practice, 
making it important to exclude. A syrinx could cause a similar 
syndrome but is much less common. Another common mimicker 
is a cervical myelopathy associated with cervical spine degenera -
tion and superimposed length-dependent neuropathy. The my -
elopathy would cause UMN findings in the upper limbs, but in 
the lower limbs the neuropathy often blunts reflexes to make 
them normal or reduced. In this case, the LMN findings are distal 
to the UMN findings, and the rule for rostral findings of UMN 
signs discussed earlier is not helpful. In both situations, the  
sensory complaints and findings are often clearly evident. A useful 
general rule is that surgically correctable myelopathies or radicu -
lopathies related to cervical spondylosis usually have more promi-
nent sensory signs or symptoms. These clinical possibilities are 
both easily excluded with an MRI of the cervical spine. Other rare 
possible causes of a combined UMN/LMN presentation include 
spinal dural arteriovenous malformations, hereditary motor 
neuropathy, hereditary spastic paraparesis, and other inherited 
disorders.
A patient with signs, symptoms, and testing consistent with 
LMN injury without clear UMN involvement has a greater num-
ber of possible causes that need to be excluded. For instance, 
myasthenia gravis, other NMJ disorders, and MMN are impor -
tant because these are treatable diseases. PMA, adult-onset SMA, 
inclusion body myositis, HD, and KD are other possibilities. 
Electrodiagnostic testing is a critical part of the evaluation, which 
needs to be designed to address potential mimickers. For instance, 
an electrodiagnostic study may need to include multiple motor 
NCS with multiple stimulation sites to investigate possible con-
duction block, as evidence for MMN. Repetitive stimulation and 
occasionally SFEMG may be considerations. Clinicians need to 
be wary of false positives as a source of diagnostic confusion in 
patients with ALS.
A patient with primarily or exclusively UMN signs has quite a 
different list of possible diagnoses. These include inflammatory 
conditions, such as multiple sclerosis and neuromyelitis optica. 
These should be excluded by the rate of progression, sensory com-
plaints, and MRI of the brain and spinal cord. Other myelopathies 
must be considered, including compressive, malignant, vitamin 
B12 deficiency, copper deficiency, and syringomyelia—all of which 
can be easily excluded with imaging and laboratory testing. When 
imaging and laboratory testing are negative, PLS is high in the 
differential, but hereditary causes, such as hereditary spastic para-
paresis, Silver syndrome (hereditary motor neuropathy V), or a 
female carrier of X-linked adrenoleukodystrophy/adrenomyelo-
neuropathy, can remain as possibilities.
Patients with predominantly bulbar symptoms have a shorter 
list of alternate diagnoses. Myasthenia gravis, KD, and myopa -
thies such as oculopharyngeal muscular dystrophy are primary 
considerations. EMG testing is critical and needs to include

Findings of NMJ abnormalities on electrodiagnostic testing

Diagnosing ALS must be done with caution and usually con -
firmed by a clinician with expertise in MND (Box 41.4). There is 
a high price for both missing the diagnosis and giving it falsely. 
Giving the diagnosis of ALS incorrectly (a false-positive diagnosis) 
is estimated to occur in 5% to 10% of cases. 48,183 About half of 
these cases had a treatable diagnosis, usually MMN or a cervical 
spondylotic myelopathy.48 KD was missed in nearly 15% of false-
positive ALS diagnoses. Facial fasciculations and a family history 
were both evident in 75% of cases and were paramount in making 
the correct diagnosis. Gynecomastia and tremor, findings ex -
pected in KD, were both only seen in about half of patients and 
were less reliable than might be expected.183 Clinicians should be 
on the lookout for red flags that the diagnosis of ALS is incorrect, 
including development of atypical symptoms or a failure to prog-
ress as expected.48,183
Missing the diagnosis of ALS (a false-negative diagnosis) also 
has consequences and is significantly more common. Nearly half 
of patients who are ultimately diagnosed with ALS were initially 
misdiagnosed.21 The most common incorrect diagnoses were lum-
bar and cervical stenosis. Spondylosis is very common in this  
age group and is seen to some extent in most patients who are 
Key Points in the Diagnosis of 
Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis
History
• Focal weakness without pain or sensory complaints is suspicious for  
amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS)
• Patients with sensory complaints or isolated fasciculations rarely have or 
develop ALS
Physical Examination
• Upper motor neuron (UMN) and lower motor neuron (LMN) findings in the 
same limb are very suspicious for ALS
• Fasciculations in isolation are common in healthy patients and in many 
LMN processes
• In combination with an abnormal examination or electromyography (EMG), 
fasciculations are highly suspicious for ALS or other motor neuron diseases 
(MNDs)
• When seen with otherwise normal examination findings and EMG, fascicu -
lations are almost always benign
Testing
• Electrodiagnostic testing is the cornerstone of identification of LMN loss in 
all MNDs, but diagnosis of ALS requires clinical correlation with examination 
features of UMN involvement
• No laboratory or imaging test can make the diagnosis of ALS in isolation, 
but genetic testing can identify pathogenic gene mutations associated with 
familial ALS and in some cases of sporadic ALS
• In a patient with clinical features of ALS, no diagnostic test can rule out ALS
• Most patients suspected of having ALS should undergo testing to exclude 
mimicking conditions
• Some testing results can be nonspecific. For example, patients with ALS  
often have mildly elevated creatine kinase and occasionally elevated  
anti GM-1
• In most patients, imaging of the brain and cervical spine are particularly 
important to exclude other causes of UMN or LMN dysfunction
• BOX 41.4 ultimately felt to have ALS. The provider must therefore be cau -
tious to avoid quickly assuming a causal relationship between 
common minor findings of spinal degeneration and often dra -
matic neurologic findings that are disproportionate and are poorly 
correlated. Unfortunately, around 10% of patients ultimately  
diagnosed with ALS had spinal surgery before the ultimate diag -
nosis of ALS—an example of the cost of this mistake. 20 Other 
common misdiagnoses include cerebrovascular disease in about 
10% and “nothing wrong” in around 15%.20 A total of 30% of 
patients ultimately diagnosed with ALS had an unnecessary sur -
gery for an incorrect diagnosis. 20 An estimated cost for misdiag -
nosis is $30,000. 20 A loss of faith in the medical system and the 
emotional toll for patients and families are harder to quantify.
It is therefore difficult to decide when exactly the suspected 
diagnosis of ALS should be given. A level of certainty needs to be 
established, and a particularly high degree of certainty that a treat-
able diagnosis is not being missed is critical. Waiting until there is 
absolutely no question of the diagnosis can have negative conse -
quences as well. Giving the diagnosis early has psychological, 
economic, and medical benefit. 8 The manner in which the diag -
nosis is given is important as well and has lasting consequences to 
the patient. Experts recommend that a doctor who knows the 
patient give the diagnosis in person with distractions, such as the 
cell phone and incoming interruptions from staff, eliminated and 
with 45 to 60 minutes available. It is helpful to begin with what 
the patient knows to determine the best way to communicate 
with the patient. If family is available for the clinic visit and the 
patient is agreeable to having family be part of the discussion, this 
is usually very beneficial. The diagnosis and what it will mean to 
the patient should be rolled out in a stepwise manner, making 
sure each step is understood before moving on. Providing addi -
tional written information or a recommended website is impor -
tant. It is most important that the patient be assured in words and 
in actions that support is at hand, and setting up a follow-up visit 
in 2 to 4 weeks before the patient leaves the office reinforces the 
provider’s commitment to the patient. Common mistakes include 
withholding the diagnosis, delivering it without empathy, and not 
giving enough information. The patient needs to be left with 
hope, and phrases like “there is nothing we can do” are inaccurate 
and unproductive. Giving the diagnosis with improper technique 
can lead to a sense of abandonment, erode the patient-physician 
relationship, and affect the patient’s psychological adjustment to 
bereavement.8
Genetic counseling and testing should be considered for pa -
tients with newly diagnosed ALS, even in those without a known 
family history. For patients with a first- or second-degree family 
member with ALS or FTD, the yield of finding a highly pene -
trant, causative mutation is 60% to 70%. 165 The clinical imple -
mentation and usefulness of genetic testing is rapidly evolving. 
Specifically, genetic therapy for SOD1-related fALS is now clini -
cally available. This makes genetic testing crucial for identifying 
potentially treatable forms of ALS, as new treatments targeting 
specific mutations are developed and tested in clinical trials. In 
some cases, if a specific genetic mutation is identified, this may 
help provide prognosis and information about the potential rate 
of disease progression. The information gained through genetic 
testing may be useful for unaffected relatives. Nevertheless, 
respecting the right of an asymptomatic relative’s desire not to know 
the possible genetic risk needs to be respected. Some also advocate 
genetic testing for those without a known affected family mem -
ber. The likelihood of finding a known causative mutation in 
sALS is 11%, but for those with ALS and FTD it approaches

ALS Functional Assessment
The revised version of the ALSFRS (ALSFRS-R) is the most com-
monly used tool to assess functional deficits in ALS longitudi -
nally.14,32 This disease-specific scale consists of 12 items grouped 
into four domains that evaluate bulbar symptoms, limb and trunk 
functionality, respiratory symptoms, and the necessity for inter -
ventions such as percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG), 
noninvasive ventilation (NIV), or tracheostomy with invasive 
ventilation.32 Although assessments were originally conducted 
exclusively through in-person and telephone interviews with 
health care professionals, self-assessments and online question -
naires are now widely used. 101,104,135 The ALSFRS-R, initially  
developed for clinical trials, has become a standard tool in ALS 
research and practice because of its strong correlation with disease 
progression and survival. However, a significant challenge is that 
individuals with identical total scores may have different disease 
stages or prognoses. This issue, known as multidimensionality, 
arises from combining diverse subscales such as bulbar, motor, 
and respiratory functioning.67
ALS Treatment
Disease-modifying medications. Three medications are cur -
rently available for disease modification in the treatment of ALS: 
riluzole, edaravone, and tofersen. Riluzole and edaravone have 
been shown to slow the progression of sALS and are approved for 
clinical use. T ofersen is specifically available for fALS related to 
SOD1 mutations. Generally, the treatment of ALS variants in -
volves using disease-modifying agents approved for sALS.
Riluzole. Multiple studies have shown riluzole to be safe and 
effective in slowing the progression of the disease. 128 The precise 
mechanism of effect of riluzole is unclear. Riluzole reduces mor -
tality 23% at 6 months and 15% at 12 months and appears to 
prolong survival by around 4 months. 182 There is a clear dose ef -
fect.8 The best benefit-to-risk ratio is achieved at the standard dose 
of 50 mg twice daily. There are few serious side effects. The most 
common adverse reactions are dizziness, asthenia, and gastrointes-
tinal disorders. Liver function test abnormalities are common and 
mild, with significant elevations of over five times the normal 
limit seen in less than 5% of patients. 111 Routine monitoring of 
liver function tests is recommended. Our typical protocol includes 
testing at baseline, monthly for 3 months, and then every 
3 months for 1 year. Riluzole should be given as early as possible 
because the life-prolonging effect seems to be greater when started 
earlier.8 Expense is the most common barrier to its use; other bar-
riers include a perceived lack of efficacy, lack of information about 
the drug, concern about side effects, and in some cases the treat -
ing physician recommending against it. 26 It is paramount that 
proper information is provided so that patients can make well-
informed decisions.
Edaravone. This free radical scavenger was originally developed 
for the treatment of stroke that was shown to have efficacy in a 
double-blind, placebo-controlled trial of over 200 patients with 
ALS.1 There was no statistically significant difference in the pri -
mary endpoints of the trial, but a subgroup of the patients treated 
seemed to benefit, leading to a second smaller trial of patients with 
characteristics similar to those of the subgroup that benefitted in 
the first trial. In this select group of 130 patients, a significant 
slowing of the disease by approximately 30% was seen over a 
6-month period, which has generated great enthusiasm. In spite 
of a sizable effect and favorable side effect profile, the interest in 
the patient community has been met with caution in the medical 
community because the results are based on one small trial of a 
select group of patients with no benefit in a broader spectrum of 
patients with ALS. 1 This group that benefited represents a small 
proportion of ALS patients; these patients all had mild to moder-
ate dysphagia, fine and gross motor dysfunction, were treated 
within 2 years of symptom onset, and had normal respiratory 
function. However, survival data from randomized clinical trials 
are lacking, and retrospective findings from other studies are in -
consistent.193 The originally approved form of edaravone is deliv-
ered intravenously, but the oral formulation medication has been 
preferred over injections or infusions due to fewer office visits and 
the avoidance of injection-site reactions and other side effects.11,71 
The recommended oral dosage is 105 mg (5 mL) every morning 
for 14 days, followed by a 14-day drug-free period. For subse -
quent treatment cycles, the same dosage is administered on 10 of 
14 days, followed by another 14-day drug-free period. Oral eda -
ravone should be taken in the morning on an empty stomach after 
overnight fasting, with no food or drink (except water) for 1 hour. 
Patients should fast for 8 hours before each dose if they have con-
sumed a high-fat meal, 4 hours if they have consumed a low-fat 
meal, or 2 hours if they have consumed a caloric supplement. 
Additionally, oral edaravone can be administered orally or through 
a nasogastric (NG) or PEG tube.73,153
T ofersen. Genetic-based therapies are an emerging area in ALS 
therapeutics. As genetic causes of fALS are determined, this opens 
possibilities for novel therapies targeting genetic mechanisms of 
MNDs. The first genetic-based therapy for fALS, tofersen 
(BIIB067) for treating SOD1-ALS, was granted accelerated ap -
proval in 2023. 10 T ofersen is an antisense oligonucleotide de -
signed to target and reduce the production of the SOD1 protein, 
which is associated with ALS development.130 The recommended 
intrathecal dose of tofersen is 100 mg/15 mL per administration, 
with initiation of treatment consisting of three loading doses ad -
ministered at 14-day intervals, followed by a maintenance dose 
every 28 days thereafter. In the phase 3 trial (VALOR) involving 
108 ALS patients, tofersen did not demonstrate a significant dif -
ference in the decline on ALSFRS-R compared with placebo. 
However, secondary endpoints indicated a reduction in CSF 
SOD1 protein and plasma neurofilament levels in the faster-  
progressing subgroup. The open-label extension study suggested 
that earlier initiation of tofersen might lead to better outcomes. 
Common adverse events included lumbar puncture–related ad -
verse events, headache, pain in the arms or legs, falls, and back 
pain.131 Further evaluation is needed to fully understand the effi -
cacy and long-term effects of tofersen in ALS treatment. As a  
result, a phase 3 study (ATLAS) is currently underway.22
Rehabilitation, symptomatic and medical treatment. Strate-
gies for rehabilitation must be designed for the targeted patient 
population based on disease impact and natural history. In par -
ticular, the progression of ALS makes prescribing rehabilitation 
interventions for patients difficult because of a changing func -
tional status. Additionally, the combination of spasticity and 
severe focal weakness creates a challenge for those trying to 
maximize function and ease care in this population.
Stages of Rehabilitation.  Patients with ALS and primarily  
spinal involvement tend to progress over six stages with common 
clusters of rehabilitation needs in each, though individuals can

vary considerably and straddle stages rather than fall neatly into 
one.174 Outlining these stages and their challenges helps the pro-
vider anticipate the rehabilitation treatments they will likely be 
discussing at the next visit.
Stage 1 includes patients who are ambulatory and fully inde -
pendent, with mild weakness or clumsiness. They need only to 
maintain active range of motion (ROM) and carry on with nor -
mal activities of daily living, but they can benefit from strengthen-
ing unaffected muscles. It is reasonable to strengthen all muscles, 
including affected muscles as long as the provider takes caution to 
prevent overwork damage (see later).
Patients in stage 2 remain ambulatory and independent but 
have moderate weakness. Functional impairment may be severe in 
some areas despite preserved overall function (Fig. 41.2). They 
often benefit from ankle-foot orthoses to compensate for foot 
drop. However, if the patient has plantar flexion spasticity, a  
dorsiflexion assist can encourage clonus and should be avoided. 
Anterior trim lines add weight but can provide medial-lateral

nosed with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis with otherwise retained bulbar 
and ambulatory function. The patient is unable to voluntarily grip the pen 
to sign a consent form.
stability for those who require it. A wrist-hand orthosis to assist 
with wrist drop is often helpful. An opponens splint can be used 
to accommodate lack of thumb opposition because of thenar 
muscle weakness (Fig. 41.3). Hand weakness also commonly 
makes buttoning difficult, and assistive devices to ease this task are 
helpful. Focal weakness of the cervical extensor muscles leading to 
head drop or chin-on-chest deformity is a common problem in 
ALS. Cervical orthoses to stabilize the cervical spine may help 
provide some relief but are sometimes poorly tolerated. Work 
simplification and input from an occupational therapist can be 
useful at this stage. Exercise prescription is similar to stage 1.
Stage 3 includes patients who remain ambulatory but have 
severe weakness in selected muscle groups. A common problem 
that arises at this stage is the inability to rise from a chair. Lift 
chairs and elevated toilet seats can mitigate this problem. Ambula-
tion aids such as a walker can be helpful, and a manual wheelchair 
for long trips is often necessary and facilitates socialization. This 
should be custom fitted to provide pressure relief and support the 
spine, but financial and insurance constraints must be considered 
when determining the most appropriate type of durable medical 
equipment. At this stage, deep breathing exercises are helpful for 
those who develop mild respiratory muscle weakness.
Stage 4 includes patients who are nonambulatory but remain 
independent. Electrically powered chairs are usually appropriate. 
Strengthening exercises are usually limited to any unaffected 
muscles, but ROM exercises for other areas are also important. A 
hospital bed facilitates bed mobility.
Stage 5 is entered when the patient is no longer independent. 
T ransfer assistance with devices such as Hoyer lifts is generally 
required. Family education is a large part of the care of the pa-
tient. A wheeled shower chair is often helpful for these patients. 
A lack of mobility makes pressure relief measures in the wheel -
chair and bed higher priorities.
At stage 6, patients are completely immobile, dependent, and 
require maximal assistance from skilled caregivers. Proper posi -
tioning is important to keep patients as comfortable as possible 
and prevent pressure ulcers. Palliative care becomes the focus of 
treatment.
Spasticity. Spasticity can have a significant impact on the func-
tion of patients with predominantly UMN involvement. T reat -
ment of spasticity in patients with ALS is similar to other diseases

of the central nervous system but with some important differ -
ences. Physical therapy has been shown to reduce spasticity in 
patients with ALS; oral baclofen is commonly used but has not 
been shown to have clear benefit. 57,145 Intrathecal baclofen has 
been used successfully in ALS patients,121 but not all are appropri-
ate candidates, specifically those with rapid progression or very 
advanced disease. Botulinum toxin can be useful in treating spe -
cific muscle groups as in other disorders, but it can lead to gener-
alized weakness that can be more problematic in a patient with 
ALS than it is for someone with a traumatic brain injury or 
stroke.127 Tizanidine, dantrolene, gabapentin, and benzodiaze -
pines are commonly used but have not been specifically studied in 
ALS.173 Benzodiazepines carry the additional benefit of helping 
treat anxiety, but they also carry the additional risk of respiratory 
depression. Dantrolene does not share this risk, but some advo -
cate avoiding it in patients with ALS.
Communication. Patients with ALS can experience communi-
cation difficulties that may eventually lead to the inability to com-
municate independently. Dysarthria can result from both UMN 
and LMN dysfunction or a combination. 173 Other barriers pa -
tients with ALS face in effective communication include hyperna-
sal speech caused by air leaking though the nose, hypophonia, and 
coexistent cognitive impairments that may occur in disorders such 
as combined ALS-FTD.
Effective communication is a major factor in the quality of life in 
patients with ALS and should be managed aggressively.8,138 Many 
patients elect to withdraw support when they become “locked-in” 
and unable to communicate, rather than when they lose a particular 
physical function. In addition to the inability to indicate needs, the 
main loss patients with impaired communication experience is of 
social closeness with their partner and family. 138,173 Given this, 
patients should be routinely monitored by a speech-language pa -
thologist to optimize communication and monitor changing needs 
during the course of the disease.8 T reatment options for impaired 
communication include palatal lifts or palatal augmentation pros-
thesis to decrease hypernasality.8 As communication becomes more 
limited, augmentative and alternative communications systems 
(AAC) are useful. These substantially improve the patient’s quality of 
life.8 AAC devices range from pointing boards to more complex eye-
gaze devices or brain-computer interfaces. Some patients with ALS 
will utilize voice or message banking. In some cases, these services 
can be used to create a synthesized voice on AAC equipment that 
sounds similar to the patient’s own voice. Patients should be encour-
aged to consider this as soon as possible after their diagnosis so that 
they can use their strongest possible voice ( https://www.als.org/
navigating-als/resources/fyi-voice-preservation).
Dysphagia. Dysphagia is a common symptom in ALS. It con-
tributes to malnutrition, poses a risk for aspiration, and can be a 
major source of anxiety. Dysphagia usually begins with thin liq -
uids, and using thickener is a simple early solution. When dyspha-
gia with solids begins, smaller, more frequent meals can be help -
ful. Moistening and softening solids can also make swallowing 
easier.173 A speech therapist is a valuable team member and can 
teach and reinforce helpful strategies such as supraglottic swallow-
ing, postural changes and using a chin tuck. 8 Ultimately with 
disease progression, the failing swallowing mechanism needs to be 
bypassed if the patient is going to get safe and adequate nutrition.
Nutrition. Proper management of nutrition is one of the most 
important jobs of a physician managing a patient with ALS. 
Without intervention, eventually patients with ALS will not get 
enough calories, and this problem magnifies as the disease ad -
vances.100 This is caused by a combination of factors: bulbar and 
masticatory muscle weakness, dysphagia, fatigue, and slow eating. 
Patients with ALS also have increased resting energy expenditure.7 
Weight loss is therefore common in patients with ALS. Almost 
three-quarters of ambulatory patients consume less than the rec -
ommended daily allowance of calories, and one-quarter have lost 
more than 10% of their body weight. 177 Malnutrition is propor -
tional to the degree of dysphagia and is a major predictor of death 
in patients with ALS. 53,177 Recruiting the help of a dietician can 
help patients maintain their calorie intake. Common interven -
tions include modifications of food and liquid consistency, the 
chin-tuck maneuver, and high-caloric supplements. Carbonated 
liquids and ice-cold liquids are more easily consumed for some 
patients.7 Calorie intake and weight loss should be a part of every 
follow-up visit. The initial strategies mentioned earlier can relieve 
these symptoms in some patients with early ALS, but eventually 
to get proper nutrition and prolong survival, more definitive mea-
sures need to be taken.
An NG tube can provide greater nutrition to patients with 
dysphagia but is uncomfortable and can lead to aspiration if im -
properly placed. A PEG tube is a permanent solution, and there 
are excellent data to support the use of PEG tubes in patients with 
ALS. Use of a PEG tube helps stabilize body weight and probably 
prolongs survival, though it is not clear if it improves quality of 
life.102,122,129 Indications for placement of a PEG tube in a patient 
with ALS include decreased caloric intake with weight loss greater 
than 10% of baseline, dehydration, and meals limited by dyspha-
gia or taking greater than 30 minutes. 173 Studies show improved 
outcomes if PEG placement is performed before the FVC drops 
below 50% of the predicted value. 102 PEG tubes do not reduce 
the rate of aspiration pneumonia; as such, this is not an indication 
for placement.173 Risks of PEG tube placement include tube dis -
lodgement, benign pneumoperitoneum, parietal hematoma, peri-
tonitis, gastrointestinal bleeding, gastrocolic fistula, and death. 52 
The overall risk of complications for PEG tube placement in pa -
tients with ALS is 6% to 10%.102 An alternative is a radiologically 
inserted gastrostomy (RIG) tube. The risks and survival benefits 
are the same as a PEG tube, but because it can be performed 
under local or light sedation (as opposed to deep sedation or gen-
eral anesthesia), it can be done in patients with a lower FVC. 170 
Home parenteral nutrition has been used for some patients with 
late-stage ALS.7
Despite the benefits of a PEG or RIG tube, most patients with 
severe dysphagia do not use them.26 There are a variety of barriers 
that keep appropriate patients with ALS from using PEG or RIG 
tubes. Physicians recommend a PEG tube in less than half of the 
patients in whom it is indicated. 26 Less than half of the patients 
to whom it is recommended actually elect to have it placed. 26 
When the physician brings up PEG tube placement to a patient 
with ALS, it is often received by the patient as a sign that things 
are going badly and death is near. This often leads to a delay in the 
discussion, at which point the risk of placing the PEG increases. 
Many patients ultimately decline a PEG tube because they are 
uncomfortable with the idea. Importantly, one study showed that 
over one-third of patients who did not receive a PEG tube lacked 
information about the role and potential benefits of a PEG tube 
or erroneously felt their swallowing was adequate. 26 Ultimately, 
many patients will refuse a PEG or RIG tube even with all the 
proper information, but the provider owes the patient the oppor-
tunity to make an informed decision.
Patients with ALS frequently look to alternative medicine for 
solutions that allopathic medicine is not providing. This is par -
ticularly true regarding nutrition. Nearly 80% of patients use

none have shown benefit. Vitamin E and creatine have been
