with ALS.128
Sialorrhea. Sialorrhea in patients with ALS can be socially 
disabling and predispose them to oral infections. 8 It is caused by 
impaired handling of saliva rather than overproduction; in fact, 
patients with ALS actually salivate less than healthy patients. 36 A 
home suction machine is something most patients find helpful. 
Multiple medications are commonly used to control saliva pro -
duction in ALS. Amitriptyline is cheap, effective, and can be 
helpful in treating a concomitant mood disorder or pseudobulbar 
affect. Typical dose is 25 to 50 mg twice to three times daily. 8 
Atropine drops 0.5 or 1% sublingually three or four times daily 
can be used. Other treatments include nebulized or intravenous 
glycopyrrolate, oral or transdermal scopolamine, and benztro -
pine.8 Side effects include constipation, urinary retention, confu -
sion, and sedation. T reatment can also lead to excessive drying of 
the nasopharynx and thickening of the mucus deep in the throat. 
Botulinum toxin type A given every 3 months at a dose starting 
at 7.5 units into each parotid gland has been used effectively 188; 
however, serious side effects, including jaw dislocation, have been 
reported.180 Radiation treatment is a fast, safe, inexpensive, and  
effective option but infrequently used. 8 Side effects are mild and 
temporary. Surgical treatments for sialorrhea are available but not 
generally recommended.8
Respiratory Insufficiency. This is very rarely the presenting 
complaint for patients with ALS, though symptoms exist on pre -
sentation in around 25% of patients when specifically investi -
gated. For the remaining majority, no respiratory symptoms are 
noted for some time after diagnosis. These symptoms can include 
fatigue, dyspnea, orthopnea, and morning headaches. Even 
though many patients have no respiratory symptoms, subclinical 
respiratory insufficiency is frequent. Up to 85% of patients with 
ALS will have an abnormal FVC at presentation.62,82 The majority 
of patients with less than 50% predicted FVC will have mild or  
no respiratory symptoms, even though NIV is both advanta -
geous and covered by Medicare.82 For this reason, experts recom-
mend routine assessment of respiratory physiologic parameters in 
patients with ALS at every visit, regardless of the presence of 
symptoms.
There are several different parameters that can be used to assess 
and monitor respiratory function. FVC is the most commonly 
utilized parameter.128 The difference between the upright and su-
pine FVC correlates best with orthopnea, which is the symptom 
most closely associated with quality of life. 25,82 The maximal in -
spiratory pressure (MIP) decreases earlier than upright FVC. 82 
The sniff transdiaphragmatic pressure is most accurate at predict-
ing hypercapnia (90% sensitive, 87% specific). 128 The sniff nasal 
pressure (SNP) is useful in a number of ways. It has greater pre -
dictive power and is more reliably recorded at later stages of dis -
ease than both FVC and MIP .128 At a threshold of less than 32% 
predicted, SNP is 81% sensitive and 85% specific for predicting 
hypercarbic respiratory failure. 82 In spite of this, FVC and MIP 
are the most established respiratory parameters and are currently 
used by most experts as well as Medicare to justify NIV . Unfortu-
nately, no parameters can predict respiratory insufficiency in the 
many patients with ALS and bulbar dysfunction. 8,115
At some point, patients with ALS will need to decide if they 
wish to use respiratory support. Those who choose respiratory 
support usually begin with NIV . NIV is delivered through a mask 
or mouthpiece without the need for a tracheostomy. This can be 
delivered with either a bilevel positive airway pressure machine or 
a portable ventilator connected to a mask. Both are portable and 
serve the same basic purpose. Portable ventilators have many more 
settings and can be used when and if the patient decides to un -
dergo tracheostomy and invasive ventilation. Both improve respi-
ratory function and quality of life. 128 Advantages to NIV include 
improved quality of life parameters, such as energy, vitality, dys -
pnea, somnolence, depression, sleep quality, physical fatigue, 
concentration problems, and cognitive function. 128,142,173 It pro -
longs survival 205 days, though it is not clear if this applies to 
those with severe bulbar involvement.8,128 It also slows the rate of 
respiratory decline from -2.2% per month before treatment to 
-1.1% after initiation. 128 NIV delays the need for tracheostomy 
and mechanical ventilation but does not appear to add additional 
burden to caregivers. 8,12,173 Most patients who have undergone 
NIV would choose it again and recommend it to other patients 
with ALS.8,12,128,173
Thresholds for beginning NIV vary. Currently, Medicare re -
quires an FVC of less than 50% or MIP less than 260 cm H2O 
or PaCO 2 of 45 mm Hg or higher. The American College of 
Chest Physicians adds a criteria of nocturnal oximetry of 88% or 
less for 5 consecutive minutes. 3 Early NIV , beginning with noc-
turnal oximetry with more than 15 desaturation events per hour, 
has been shown in nonrandomized trials to confer an 11-month 
survival benefit compared with controls using NIV at the thresh -
olds listed earlier. 128 The setting for beginning use of NIV can 
vary. Use of NIV for greater than 4 hours per day offers a survival 
advantage of 7 months and a slower rate of respiratory decline 
when compared with those using it less. 128 A typical initial pre -
scription for NIV would include the International Classification of 
Diseases (10th edition) G12.21 diagnosis for ALS and would in -
clude documentation of an FVC less than 50% or MIP less than  
260 cm H2O or PaCO2 45 mm Hg or higher.82
In spite of the benefits of NIV , many patients are never offered 
it.82 About one-third of those who use NIV start it emergently. 82 
This is not ideal, but benefits of NIV can still be achieved when 
started emergently.82 Eventually, all patients will require more re-
spiratory support and secretion management, and they will face 
the decision to undergo tracheostomy and invasive ventilation 
with a mechanical ventilator. This decision is often made urgently, 
when a patient is intolerant of NIV or unable to clear secretions.82 
T riggers to consider invasive ventilation on a nonurgent basis in-
clude use of NIV longer than 12 hours per day or intolerance of 
NIV with an FVC less than 50% or symptoms of dyspnea. 82 
T racheostomy and invasive ventilation can substantially prolong 
life; in one series, 47% of patients survived more than 5 years. 173 
Much of this time was in a locked-in state where patients were 
unable to communicate. 173 Yet, patients report an 80% positive 
perception, would have it again, and would recommend it to  
another patient with ALS. 82 Unlike NIV , there is a significant 
burden of invasive ventilation on caregivers, many of whom 
would not endorse invasive ventilation so strongly or at all. Only 
50% of caregivers would consider it for themselves if they were to 
need it, and 30% of caregivers report a worse quality of life than 
the patient. 82 Invasive ventilation via tracheostomy is costly as 
well, imposing an estimated $150,000 or more per year on the 
health care system.
Diaphragmatic pacers have been shown to be of no benefit or 
may even decrease survival in patients with ALS. 77,136,194 Oxygen 
therapy should almost never be used in patients with ALS. It 
worsens respiratory symptoms as well as hypercapnia and can lead 
to hypercapnic coma or respiratory arrest.13,82 Flow rates as low as

Bronchial secretions are often problematic and represent a 
major source of anxiety as well as being a negative prognostic 
factor for those on NIV .
156 First-line treatments include a por-
table home suction device and room humidifier. Mucolytics such 
as N-acetylcysteine, 200 to 400 mg three times daily, can also be 
helpful. Nebulized saline, beta antagonists, anticholinergic bron -
chodilators, and mucolytics have been used in various combina -
tions with success. Surgical cricopharyngeal myotomy has been 
used to relieve cricopharyngeal spasm and severe bronchial secre -
tions.
8 High-frequency chest wall oscillation, used effectively in 
cystic fibrosis, has been shown not to work in two randomized 
trials, and the American Academy of Neurology indicates there 
are insufficient data to support or refute its use in patients with 
ALS.
82,128 Breath stacking is a technique that involves multiple 
sequential inhalations before exhalation. It can increase inspira -
tory volume up to 20% and increase the peak cough flow (PCF) 
significantly.
82 Patients can do this without assistance if they can 
close their glottis between inspiratory efforts; otherwise, a care -
giver can use a resuscitation bag connected to a mask. A manual 
cough-assist can be performed by a caregiver. This consists of a 
caregiver leaning over a partially supine patient and applying 
downward pressure on the chest with one forearm, while thrust -
ing on the abdomen with the other hand during expiration.
82 
The combination of manual cough assist and breath stacking is 
more effective than breath stacking alone.
82 Manual cough assist 
should be avoided in patients with a Greenfield filter and used 
with caution in patients with recent abdominal surgery, abdomi -
nal feeding tubes, and those at high risk of rib fracture.
82 If a 
patient has a vital capacity less than 340 mL or PCF less than  
90 L/min, then transitioning to mechanical insufflation-exsufflation 
is typical.
A mechanical cough-assist device (mechanical insufflator-  
exsufflator [MI-E]) can be very effective via facemask and has 
been shown to be effective in uncontrolled trials.
86,167 It works 
by providing positive airway pressure for a few seconds, then 
suddenly switching to negative pressure to simulate the pres-
sures created in a healthy cough. T reatments usually consist of 
four to six insufflation-exsufflation cycles in a sequence.  
Sequences are separated by 30 seconds of rest. Four to six se -
quences make up a treatment, and patients usually receive three 
to four treatments per day.
82 This can increase to one to two 
treatments per hour in the setting of a respiratory tract infec -
tion.82 The combination of NIV and MI-E has been shown to 
reduce hospitalization and improve oxygen saturation in pa -
tients with neuromuscular disorders, including ALS. 82 A typical 
prescription would read 82:
Cough-assist device, 4 treatments per day and as needed, 5 coughing 
cycles per sequence followed by 30 seconds of rest, with 5 sequences 
for each treatment, beginning at 115 cm H
2O insufflation pressure/ 
215 cm H2O exsufflation pressure, increased in 5–10 cm H2O incre-
ments, to a target 140/240 cm H2O and oxygen saturation .94%.
Presently, this is covered by Medicare for patients with ALS 
and a PCF of less than 300 to 350 L/min (5–6 L/sec). 82 It is less 
effective for patients with significant bulbar dysfunction, in 
whom a tracheostomy is often a better solution for secretion man-
agement. Contraindications include bullous emphysema, pneu -
mothorax, and pneumomediastinum.
82
Mood Disorders. It is important for treating providers to dis -
cuss symptoms of mood disorders with patients (and families of 
patients) with ALS because treatment can lead to improved qual -
ity of life.
114 T ricyclic antidepressants and selective serotonin 
reuptake inhibitors are often used. T ricyclic antidepressants have 
the added benefit of treating sialorrhea, pseudobulbar affect, and 
insomnia, but they are complicated by many side effects. Anxiety 
can be a particular problem when respiratory insufficiency devel -
ops, prompting use of benzodiazepines. They are effective but can 
suppress respiratory drive. Counseling is helpful to many patients 
and an integral part of most multidisciplinary clinics. Support 
groups through an organization such as the Muscular Dystrophy 
Association or groups specific to ALS, SMA, and other MNDs 
should be encouraged to patients who are open to the idea.
Pseudobulbar Affect. Pseudobulbar affect is pathologic weep -
ing, laughing, and yawning that is inappropriate or excessive to the 
emotional state of the patient resulting from UMN dysfunction. It 
can consist of laughing or crying spontaneously when there is no 
apparent reason and can be alarming and distressing to those 
around the patient. The underlying mechanism is unknown but 
may be caused by loss of frontal lobe inhibition of spontaneous 
brainstem-generated emotional responses. Pseudobulbar affect is 
seen in over one-half of patients with ALS with or without bulbar 
motor signs.
70 It is not a mood disorder, does not reflect the emo-
tional state of the patient, and can often be ignored. Caregivers are 
often more concerned than patients themselves but must be edu-
cated on the true nature of the expression. Multiple antidepres -
sants and even levodopa and lithium have been used.
173 A combi-
nation of dextromethorphan and quinidine is US Food and Drug 
Administration (FDA) approved for treatment of pseudobulbar 
affect and has been shown to improve both symptoms and quality 
of life.
29 It is not clear how this combination helps pseudobulbar 
affect, but dextromethorphan is a known N-methyl-D-aspartate 
antagonist. About one-quarter of patients discontinued the combi-
nation, with common but mild side effects, including nausea, 
dizziness, and somnolence. There were increases in the QT interval 
seen in trials, but these were not clinically significant.
29
Cognition. Cognitive difficulties affect up to one-half of  
patients with ALS. 7 Many of the gene mutations responsible for 
familial ALS can also cause FTD, and the reverse is also true, 
demonstrating an underlying link between the two processes. Ap-
proximately 15% of patients diagnosed with ALS will meet the 
criteria for FTD, and nearly one-third will meet criteria for mild 
cognitive or behavioral impairment.
7 Symptoms of FTD may 
include apathy, change in emotional reactivity, sleep disturbances, 
stereotypical or repetitive behaviors, poor insight or judgment, 
and loss of expressive language with relative preservation of com -
prehension. Several cognitive screens have been developed to 
rapidly assess cognition in patients with ALS. Importantly, the 
Mini-Mental State Examination often will not detect mild cogni-
tive or behavioral impairment in patients with ALS.
7 In addition 
to complicating the care of patients who may already have other 
barriers to communication, frontotemporal syndrome is a nega-
tive prognostic factor for patients with ALS. Cognitive impair -
ment can also complicate the ability to discern if a patient retains 
capacity to make end-of-life decisions.
Insomnia and Fatigue.  Insomnia is a common symptom in 
ALS, particularly later in the disease. It is likely related to multiple 
factors, including pain, respiratory difficulties, mood disorders, 
and a disturbed sleep-wake cycle. The most commonly used 
medications include amitriptyline and zolpidem.
191 Fatigue is 
another common and likely multifactorial problem affecting

General Medical Complications.  Patients with ALS are at 
greater risk for venous thromboembolic events, presumably re -
lated to immobility and respiratory dysfunction. There have not 
been studies of pharmacologic prophylaxis, but limb elevation 
and compression stockings are recommended. 7 Despite con -
cerns, no convincing evidence has been shown to support an 
increased incidence of ALS or more rapid disease progression of 
ALS with the use of statins. 99 Patients with ALS do not require 
adjustments to vaccination guidelines or antibiotic use. 99 Sur -
gery in patients with ALS is complicated because of many of the 
problems discussed earlier. A medical evaluation is recom -
mended before surgery, with particular attention to the pulmo -
nary system. 99 Regional anesthesia is generally recommended 
over general anesthesia. 99 Because of a higher incidence of post -
operative respiratory depression, extubation should be withheld 
in patients with ALS until they are fully awake after the use of 
inhalation agents. 99 Depolarizing neuromuscular blockers are 
contraindicated in patients with ALS because of prolonged neu -
romuscular blockade. 99 NIV has been useful in patients with 
respiratory involvement after having received local anesthesia. 99
Prognosis and End-of-Life Care
End-of-life issues need to be addressed early in the management 
of every patient with ALS. Delaying uncomfortable end-of-life 
conversations may be tempting for both provider and patient 
alike. This may cause family members to make critical decisions 
during a crisis when they are unaware of the patient’s true wishes. 
Establishing a health care proxy, living will, and Do Not Resusci-
tate orders helps avoid this. Clear and repeated communication of 
the patient’s wishes to caregivers and family is still important even 
if all the necessary arrangements are in place. Palliative care spe -
cialists should be involved early. 8 It is helpful to review the pa -
tient’s wishes every 6 months to ensure that documentation is 
current and compatible with the patient’s recent state of mind.
Providers often soften the blow of the initial diagnosis by mak-
ing a promise: that everything will be done to keep the patient 
comfortable. The actual medical treatment of distressing symp -
toms in the last months of life is often done too conservatively. 
One survey found that in the last month of life, on a scale of 1 to 
6 (6 representing constant suffering, 1 representing no suffering), 
caregivers gave an average rating of 4, and nearly half rated this 
final month as a 5 or 6. 173 It seems more needs to be done to 
deliver on this promise, and there may not be a more important 
task that a physician must undertake.
Common complaints in the last month of life are dyspnea, 
difficulty sleeping, choking episodes, severe pain, anxiety, confu -
sion, and depression.173 The initial management of many of these 
issues was previously discussed. Symptomatic treatment of inter -
mittent dyspnea can consist of lorazepam 0.5 to 2.5 mg sublin -
gually and morphine 2.5 mg orally or subcutaneously. 8 Chronic 
dyspnea of a moderate degree can be treated with this same  
morphine regimen four to six times daily. 8 Severe dyspnea can  
be treated with morphine intravenously or subcutaneously at  
0.5 mg/hour and titrated to effect. 8 Midazolam or diazepam can 
be used for nocturnal symptoms. 8 Pain can be treated with non -
narcotic medications but generally requires opioids, which have 
many cross benefits for dyspnea and anxiety. Benzodiazepines are 
usually used for anxiety as well as dyspnea.
Providers are often concerned about overtreating the patient, but 
palliative care experts assert that titrating doses of benzodiazepines 
and opioids to clinical symptoms will almost never result in life-
threatening respiratory depression.8 Supplemental oxygen, however, 
can suppress respiratory drive and cause hypercarbic respiratory 
failure. It should only be given to patients with symptomatic hy -
poxia. Restlessness and confusion caused by hypercapnia can be 
treated with neuroleptics such as chlorpromazine 12.5 mg every 4 to 
12 hours orally, intravenously, or as needed. Patients and caregivers 
can be reassured that death itself is peaceful. Surveys have found that 
91% of deaths caused by ALS in the United States and Canada were 
considered peaceful by caregivers.173
The prognosis for ALS is grim ( Box 41.5 ). There is a small 
(10%) subset of patients with longevity for reasons that are un -
clear. A fortunate half of these have a more benign course; the 
other half have prolonged but severe involvement. 144 Overall 
survival from the onset of symptoms averages between 2 and  
3 years, and survival after the diagnosis is made is 15 months on 
average, with significant variability between individual pa -
tients.39,83,144 The prognosis is worse with age and bulbar involve-
ment. Males survive longer than females possibly because of their 
lower incidence of bulbar involvement. 33 Malnutrition causes  
a nearly eightfold increase in the risk of death. 53 Those with  
more diffuse involvement and a more rapid rate of decline have  
a shorter survival. PEG tube placement, NIV , treatment with 
riluzole, and participation in multidisciplinary clinics improve 
survival.40,182
Spinal Muscular Atrophy
General
SMA is a genotypically and phenotypically diverse group of LMN 
disorders. The most common and well-understood form, proximal 
SMA (also called SMN-related SMA, 5q-SMA, or simply SMA), is 
an autosomal recessive lower MND with a frequency of 1/11,000 
births.154,161 Carrier frequency is about 1/50. 178 Importantly, un-
treated proximal SMA is the most common genetic cause of death 
in infants.164 Proximal SMA historically has been classified into five 
subtypes of disease, though expected disease onset and progression 
has changed with availability of new therapies (Fig. 41.4).9,58,157,199 
The most severe form, type 0, has onset before birth, with very se-
vere weakness and often joint contractures caused by diminished 
intrauterine movement (not shown in Fig. 41.4). Type 1 is the most 
common form of SMA, representing 60% to 70% of cases; it is

independently without treatment. Historically, 95% of type 1 SMA 
patients die by the age of 2 years without treatment.
23 Patients with 
type 2 have onset between 6 and 18 months of age; they achieve the 
ability to sit upright independently but are unable to ambulate 
without treatment. Patients with type 3 have an onset after 
18 months of age and are able to walk independently at least tem-
porarily. The mildest form, type 4, is marked by onset in adulthood 
with relatively mild proximal limb weakness.
157
SMA is caused by homozygous deletions or loss-of-function 
mutations of the SMN1 gene, which is located on chromosome 
5q13.113 In 95% of cases, patients have a homozygous deletion 
of the SMN1 gene, but 5% of patients will be compound hetero-
zygotes with deletion of one SMN1 allele and a missense muta -
tion of the other SMN1 allele. A second, closely related gene, 
SMN2, is retained in SMA patients in varying copy numbers. 
SMN1 and SMN2 genes encode the same SMN protein, but be -
cause of a single nucleotide C-T transition in exon 7, the SMN2 
gene in 90% of transcripts produces a shortened, unstable form 
of SMN that does not function normally and is rapidly degraded. 
In SMA patients, there is homozygous loss of both SMN1 alleles, 
and reduced amounts of full-length SMN protein are produced 
by retained copies of the SMN2 gene.
9 Consequently, SMA is not 
related to absence of the SMN protein but, rather, reduced levels. 
Given the varying copy numbers of the SMN2 gene in the general 
population, there are variable levels of full-length SMN protein 
production. Generally, SMA type 1 is associated with two copies 
of SMN2. In cross-sectional studies, the copy number generally 
correlates inversely with severity of SMA; however, it is important 
to note that SMN2 copy number cannot precisely predict severity 
in an individual patient.
31,61,118,192 The mechanism of how motor 
neuron dysfunction is related to reduced SMN levels remains 
unknown.
9
SMA Assessment
Clinical presentation. The clinical presentation of SMA in -
cludes predominantly proximal weakness and hypotonia. Reflexes 
may be absent or reduced. Sensory examination is typically nor -
mal. About 60% to 70% of cases of SMA will have onset of weak-
ness and hypotonia after birth but before 6 months of age and will 
not gain the ability to sit independently; these cases a re classified 
as type 1, but in milder forms of SMA, symptoms may not be 
noted until adulthood. Other characteristic clinical features may 
include a fine tremor (polyminimyoclonus), tongue fasciculations, 
and paradoxic abdominal movement caused by relative sparing of 
diaphragm strength. The diagnosis of proximal SMA should be 
suspected in any infant developing hypotonia and weakness.
Laboratory studies. The most efficient diagnostic strategy is 
genetic testing for homozygous deletion of the SMN1 gene, 
which can identify 95% of proximal SMA cases. Yet 5% of SMA 
patients are compound heterozygotes with a single deletion and a 
missense mutation. In such cases, SMN1 deletion testing is nor-
mal. Subsequent analysis with SMN1 dosage testing to identify a 
single SMN1 deletion and sequencing the remaining SMN1 gene 
are required to confirm the diagnosis of SMA.
Other testing. Strategie include electrodiagnosis and muscle 
biopsy, but given the availability of molecular diagnostics, such 
strategies are reserved for atypical cases or SMA not related to the 
SMN1 gene. When performed, electrodiagnosis correlates with 
clinical severity and age and shows variable features of motor 
neuron/axon loss consistent with loss of motor neuron func -
tion.
30,87,179 Sensory studies are usually normal.30,87,179
Other gene mutations have been associated with autosomal 
recessive forms of proximal SMA, including IGHMP2, which is 
associated with SMA with respiratory distress, and Gle1, which is 
associated with severe lethal congenital contracture syndrome 1 
and ultimately fetal loss of motor neurons.
81,190 Autosomal domi-
nant forms of proximal SMA have been associated with mutations 
in VAPB, TRPV4, LMNA, and recently BICD2.
45,141,147,155  
X-linked recessive SMA is associated with Ube1 mutations. 18 
Some forms of SMA are associated with a distal predominant pat-
tern of weakness and therefore have been described with the term 
distal SMA (Fig. 41.5). However, because there are notable 
phenotypic and genotypic overlaps between distal SMA and other

94,186 Chronic or adult-

to that of proximal SMA and is sometimes associated with fea -

152
SMA Treatment
Disease-modifying medications.  There are currently three 
genetic-based treatments for SMA: nusinersen, onasemnogene 
abeparvovec, and risdiplam.
162
Nusinersen. This medication was the first FDA-approved ther-
apy for all types of SMA in both pediatric and adult patients. It is 
an antisense oligonucleotide that corrects SMN2 mRNA splicing, 
leading to the production of functional SMN protein.
92,176 Be -
cause of its large size and inability to cross the blood-brain barrier, 
nusinersen is administered intrathecally. The recommended dosage 
is 12 mg per administration, starting with three loading doses 
given at 14-day intervals and a fourth after a 30-day interval. 
Maintenance doses are administered every 4 months. The most 
common side effects include lower respiratory infection, fever,

puncture syndrome. 139 In related clinical trials, nusinersen has 
demonstrated the necessary safety requirements along with signifi-
cant improvements in achieving motor milestones and stabilizing 
motor function. Overall, data from trials suggest that earlier treat-
ment initiation leads to better stabilization of motor function. 
There are still gaps in the research on nusinersen. The long-term 
efficacy of nusinersen into adulthood is yet to be demonstrated.59
Onasemnogene Abeparvovec. This gene replacement therapy 
offers a single intravenous treatment, contrasting with the fre -
quent and invasive dosing of nusinersen. It uses adeno-associated 
virus 9, a blood-brain barrier–penetrant virus, to deliver SMN1 
cDNA under a chicken beta-actin hybrid promoter to all tissues 
via the circulatory system. 66 T rials of onasemnogene abeparvovec 
found that among 12 patients who received the higher dose, 11 
could sit unassisted, speak, and feed orally, and two could walk by 
20 months of age. 123 The higher dose became the therapeutic 
standard, with all patients surviving without permanent ventila -
tion and maintaining motor milestones for 5 years. 125 However, 
significant liver toxicity was noted, with elevated serum amino -
transferases managed by prednisolone treatment. 84 Onasemno -
gene abeparvovec is now approved by the FDA for patients under 
2 years old with biallelic mutations in the SMN1 gene.84 Despite 
elevated liver enzymes in over one-half of the patients, the one-
time intravenous dose of onasemnogene abeparvovec significantly 
improved the achievement of key developmental motor mile -
stones.34,50 Data are still needed to fully understand the long-term 
effectiveness of this therapy.
Risdiplam. This daily oral medication is FDA approved for treat-
ing SMA patients age 2 months and older. It provides a less invasive 
alternative to nusinersen and onasemnogene abeparvovec. 47 As  
an SMN2 pre-mRNA splicing modifier, risdiplam increases SMN 
protein production and distributes throughout the central nervous 
system and peripheral organs, increasing SMN protein levels body-
wide.158 Risdiplam should be taken orally once daily after a meal, at 
the same time each day. Results from a phase 2/3 study on infants 
with SMA type 1 showed improved motor function, with 29%  
able to sit unassisted for 5 seconds after 12 months.46 Another phase 
2/3 study evaluated children and young adults with type 2 or 
3 SMA. By 12 months, patients treated with risdiplam had a Motor 
Function Measure 32 score 1.55 points higher than the placebo 
group, increasing to 1.83 points by 24 months. 16 The treatment 
showed less improvement over time in older individuals but was 
more effective in stabilizing symptoms in these patients. Common 
adverse reactions include fever, diarrhea, rash, mouth ulcers, arthral-
gia, urinary tract infections, upper respiratory infection, pneumonia, 
constipation, and vomiting. Risdiplam is contraindicated for pa -
tients with hepatic abnormalities.4,54 Additionally, risdiplam may 
impact male fertility, which is particularly concerning for adult male 
patients.151,162
Rehabilitation, symptomatic and medical treatment. SMN-
restoring treatments have altered the treatment landscape for pa -
tients with SMA and physiatrists treating patients with SMA. 
Earlier implementation of SMN-restoring treatments clearly 
demonstrates more pronounced improvements in motor func -
tion.42,124 Improved strategies are needed for earlier diagnosis and 
treatment, including genetic screening, to improve outcomes. 
More work is needed to optimize SMN-related therapeutics, to 
understand the long-term impact of genetic therapies, and to 
understand future needs of patients affected by SMA.
Despite the approval and availability of SMN-restoring treat -
ment, most patients continue to require some aspect of medical 
and rehabilitation management to address musculoskeletal,  
pulmonary, nutritional, and gastrointestinal complications. Re -
strictive lung disease is a major issue that should be aggressively 
managed similar to other MNDs. Pulmonary disease is the main 
source of mortality; muscle weakness can cause complications  
related to impaired ventilation and secretion management, as  
well as secondary complications such as aspiration pneumonia. 
Pulmonary complications are related to the severity of disease, 
wherein greater disease burden requires closer monitoring and 
more frequent intervention. Establishing a therapeutic relation -
ship with an experienced pulmonary specialist familiar with the 
management of SMA is critical and should occur at the time of 
initial diagnosis to anticipate future needs and prevent complica -
tions. A multidisciplinary team approach that incorporates the 
patient’s and family’s wishes, as well as the patient’s best interests, 
should guide the level of ventilatory support offered.
Bulbar weakness is common in severe forms of SMA, predis -
posing to inadequate nutritional intake. Therefore, nutritional 
support is an important aspect of supportive care, with goals of 
avoiding under or over nutritional supplementation and reducing 
the risk of aspiration pneumonia. Scoliosis is common in SMA, 
and its progression and the associated impact on pulmonary func-
tion are less severe in patients with SMA type 3 compared with 
type 2. A corset brace does not halt scoliosis progression but may 
help provide seating stability and delay surgical correction. Surgi-
cal treatment is well established in nonambulatory patients at age 
10 to 12 years with progressive scoliosis.
X-Linked Spinobulbar Muscular Atrophy 
(Kennedy Disease)
KD (or X-linked spinobulbar muscular atrophy) is an X-linked 
recessive disorder that leads to progressive limb and bulbar weak -
ness, testicular atrophy, gynecomastia, muscle cramps, and fas -
ciculations.105 KD is related to an expanded CAG trinucleotide 
repeat in the q arm of the X chromosome within the first exon of 
the androgen receptor gene. 110 Healthy individuals have 10 to  
36 repeats in this region, whereas patients with KD have 40 to  
62 repeats. Usually female carriers are unaffected, but some inves-
tigations have suggested subclinical features in manifesting female 
carriers, including muscle cramps and tremor. Electrodiagnostic 
or muscle biopsy evaluation may show features consistent with 
denervation.80 Early manifestations of the disease in affected 
males include symptoms of muscle cramps, fasciculations, and 
tremor; whereas progressive muscle loss, weakness, and aspiration 
pneumonia from bulbar involvement are evident later in the dis -
ease course. KD typically has an onset of more overt symptoms by 
the fourth or fifth decade. KD is a slowly progressive disorder, and 
therefore lifespan is not usually dramatically reduced.
Clinical examination demonstrates proximal predominant and 
bulbar muscular weakness and atrophy; however, distal involve -
ment and asymmetry can occur. This variability can sometimes 
complicate the clinical evaluation and more closely mimic the 
presentation of ALS. Key differences from ALS include absent or 
reduced reflexes, a lack of other UMN signs, often mild asymp -
tomatic sensory loss, and in some patients features of androgen 
insensitivity (e.g., gynecomastia, reduced fertility). In addition, 
patients with KD often have prominent bulbar involvement with 
profound atrophy and perioral fasciculations (Fig. 41.7).
Genetic testing for CAG repeats in the androgen receptor gene 
is diagnostic of KD, but because of a significant overlap with 
other MNDs, electrodiagnostic testing is often obtained. EMG

have shown improvement after treatment with androgen receptor 
antagonists.
96 Supportive treatment is similar to ALS without the 
need to treat complications of UMN dysfunction, such as spastic-

dysfunction.
Poliomyelitis, Postpolio Syndrome, and Acute 
Flaccid Myelitis
Poliomyelitis
Poliomyelitis is a MND caused by viral infection of the central 
nervous system resulting in the loss of anterior horn cells and 
cranial nerve nuclei. Poliomyelitis results primarily from infec -
tions with the poliovirus, a human enterovirus of the Picornaviri-
dae family. Other enteroviruses and flaviviruses, such as West Nile 
virus, are also associated with the clinical syndrome of poliomy-
elitis, but the poliovirus itself is the most widespread and well-
known cause.
91,168 There are three serotypes of the poliovirus, but 
immunity is not conferred between serotypes as a result of differ -
ing capsid proteins and antigenicity.133 There has been a dramatic 
• Fig. 41.7 Typical features of atrophy seen in bulbar and tongue muscles 
in a patient with Kennedy disease.
reduction in the annual number of cases of polio as a result of the 
development of vaccination strategies in the 1950s, and in 1988, 
the World Health Assembly formulated plans for worldwide ef -
forts to eradicate polio. These efforts have led to a dramatic reduc-
tion from over 300,000 cases worldwide to only 12 cases of wild-
type polio reported in 2023, and those were restricted to Pakistan 
and Afghanistan.
2 Circulating vaccine-derived poliovirus can oc-
cur in populations of underimmunized children from excretion of 
oral live poliovirus that then mutates and becomes pathogenic. 
Importantly, fewer than 800 cases of vaccine-derived poliomyeli -
tis have occurred over a period that would incurred an estimated 
6.5 million cases of wild-type polio without the vaccine.
2
After individuals are exposed to the virus via the fecal-oral route, 
the poliovirus has a variable incubation period, ranging from a few 
days to over a month. During this period, the virus replicates in the 
gastrointestinal tract. There is a wide spectrum of disease severity, 
ranging from asymptomatic infection to severe paralysis and death. 
Before the availability of vaccination, polio represented the most 
common cause of acute flaccid paralysis. Individuals with poliovirus 
infection are often asymptomatic but may have symptoms of head-
ache, fever, stiffness, and pain.
91 Less than 5% of infections result 
in irreversible paralysis, and in these affected individuals, mortality 
can be as high as 10% from autonomic dysfunction, circulatory 
collapse, and ventilatory muscle failure.
91
Symptoms of asymmetric flaccid paralysis and subsequent at-
rophy are more common in limb muscles but may also affect 
bulbar muscles. Rarely, transverse myelitis associated with features 
of autonomic, sensory, and sphincter dysfunction can occur. The 
clinical presentation of poliomyelitis can closely resemble other 
disorders affecting the spinal cord, muscles, and nerves.
91 There-
fore, when diagnosing, it is crucial to consider and rule out other 
potential causes of acute flaccid paralysis. Electrodiagnostic test -
ing shows features of asymmetric or multifocal denervation. CSF 
analysis demonstrates elevated protein levels, but in contrast to 
other common causes of acute flaccid paralysis such as Guillain-
Barré syndrome (GBS), there is evidence of pleocytosis. MRI can 
show features of increased T2 signal intensity in the region of the 
ventral horn of the spinal cord.
119 Molecular testing with poly -
merase chain reaction can confirm viral serotype and can deter-
mine whether the infection is vaccine derived or is a wild-type 
strain. Management of acute poliomyelitis is supportive and is 
similar to other disorders of flaccid paralysis.
Postpolio Syndrome
After paralytic poliomyelitis, the surviving motor neurons can 
compensate and, through collateral reinnervation, expand their 
territories to provide some functional recovery.
44 This phase usu-
ally maximizes within 2 years of disease; thereafter, motor system 
function stabilizes. For yet-to-be determined reasons, approxi -
mately 50% of patients with poliomyelitis develop late features of 
declining motor function, usually 30 or more years after onset, 
with onset that may be gradual or abrupt.
44 A thorough evalua-
tion is critical to exclude other disorders that may present as a 
decline in function. T reatment of postpolio syndrome is designed 
to maximize and maintain function. Therapeutic rehabilitation 
typically includes strategies to allow for energy conservation by 
both modifying the environment and delivering appropriate 
equipment needs similar to other disorders of the motor neuron.

Acute flaccid myelitis (AFM) is a syndrome first noted in the 
United States in 2014, seen primarily in children and thought to

the same family as the poliovirus. The neurologic syndrome is 
typically preceded by nonspecific viral symptoms and consists  
of patchy acute flaccid limb paralysis that evolves over 48 to  
96 hours.
140 Cranial nerve involvement is seen in about 25% of 
patients.76 Sensory findings are minimal, and in this way AFM 
resembles poliomyelitis as well as the acute motor axonal neu -
ropathy variant of GBS. The weakness seen in AFM is usually in 
a classically patchy distribution, unlike the ascending pattern of -
ten seen in GBS, and the weakness of AFM tends to progress 
more rapidly. CSF examination shows cytoalbuminologic disso -
ciation similar to GBS. The MRI findings of increased T2 signal 
in the anterior horns is the most helpful test finding to distinguish 
AFM from GBS. T reatment usually consists of high-dose corti -
costeroids, intravenous immunoglobulin, or plasma exchange. 
Maximum improvement is usually seen by 6 months, but a full 
recovery is seen in less than 20% of patients; close to 15% require 
long-term use of assistive devices for ambulation or are completely 
dependent on caregiver support.
76
Rare and Less Well-Defined Etiologies of MND
Beyond the more common forms of MND, a myriad of other 
unusual and atypical forms are less well-defined and preclude a 
detailed description within this chapter. Some of the uncommon 
forms have been attributed to paraneoplastic and idiopathic im-
mune dysregulation, other infectious agents, hereditary metabolic 
disorders, electrical injuries, and other idiopathic processes. For -
tunately, most of these are exceedingly uncommon. Because of the 
sheer rarity of MNDs as a whole, investigation into cause and 
effect vs. chance association is difficult. Search of the literature 
often reveals confusing data in this regard. One such association 
is that of disorders of the endocrine system and MND. Primary 
hyperparathyroidism may lead to a condition of MND, but in 
regard to the pathogenesis of ALS, most data suggest this associa-
tion is coincidental.
95
Hirayama Disease
HD is a self-limited disorder associated with muscle weakness and 
atrophy of the distal upper limb muscles. Several names have been 
used to describe HD, including monomelic amyotrophy, distal 
muscular atrophy of the distal upper extremity, and oblique amy-
otrophy. HD was first described in 1959 by Hirayama.
89 The 
pathogenesis of HD has been attributed to the anterior displace-
ment of the dural wall causing impaired microcirculation of the 
lower cervical spinal cord and loss of motor neurons.
41,60 The 
disorder primarily affects males during the teenage years or early 
20s, and the onset of weakness is insidious and slowly progressive. 
The natural history of the disease is associated with progression 
for 5 years or less.
75,78 Involvement may be unilateral or bilateral 
but is usually more prominent in one limb ( Fig. 41.8). Interest-
ingly, preponderance for right-sided involvement has been 
noted.
78 The phenomenon of worsening weakness with exposure 
to cold, termed cold paresis, has been frequently described in 
patients with HD.
78 Features of UMN signs, cranial nerve abnor-
malities, or incontinence of bowel or bladder are not seen, and 
sensory disturbance is usually absent or minimal.
Electrodiagnostic assessment demonstrates active and chronic 
denervation in muscles innervated by the C7-T1 myotomes with 
features of fibrillation potentials and enlarged MUAPs with de -
creased recruitment. Fasciculations are not apparent on clinical or 
electrodiagnostic examinations. MRI with neck flexion is used to 
assess for forward displacement of the posterior dural sac.
38,60,106
Because of the insidious onset, slow progression, and relatively 
uncommon nature of the disorder, there is typically significant 
delay in recognizing HD.
116 Although HD is a self-limited disor-
der, early diagnosis is critical to avoid delay in treatment and 
avoid unnecessary loss of upper limb function.
116 T reatment may 
include using a cervical collar to limit neck flexion. In patients 
with continued progression despite conservative management, 
cervical spine surgery for decompression and stabilization may be 
indicated.
116,181 Early treatment can demonstrate significant effi -
cacy, which supports the belief that the origin is related to struc -
tural dysfunction rather than a true degenerative process. 75,116
Paraneoplastic Degeneration
Paraneoplastic degeneration of the UMNs and LMNs has been 
uncommonly reported as a cause of MND. In most cases re -
ported, the link between MND and paraneoplastic degeneration 
is purely that of coincidental association. In some cases, an  
etiologic link has been suspected on the basis of a temporal rela
-
tionship between the onset of MND and cancer, the presence of 
autoantibodies, and the response to immunomodulatory treat -
ments such as intravenous immunoglobulin or corticosteroids. 
The causative relationship between cancer and the development of 
motor neuron degeneration is felt to be unlikely in most cases of

cancer and MND.65 Some authors have even suggested that para-
neoplastic processes could unmask fALS or increase the pene -
trance underlying fALS-related gene mutations. 55,72 One of the 
best defined paraneoplastic syndromes associated with MND is 
an anti-Hu syndrome.65 The clinical spectrum of anti-Hu–related 
paraneoplastic disorders includes sensory neuronopathy, cerebel -
lar ataxia, and limbic encephalitis; this overlaps with syndromes of 
multifocal involvement and syndromes of motor neuron or motor 
axonal loss, often with sensory involvement.79 The presence of an 
anti-Hu MND-like phenotype is infrequent at less than 5% of 
cases with anti-Hu syndromes. 79 Anti-Hu antibodies are not felt 
to be pathogenic but appear to be a marker of autoimmunity 
against Hu antigens. 79 T reatment includes management of the 
underlying neoplasm and in some cases immunomodulation.
Nonpoliomyelitis Infections
Infections other than poliomyelitis have been more rarely impli -
cated in the pathogenesis of MND. 85,143,166 Of these infections, 
the rare association of human immunodeficiency virus (HIV)  
infection with MND is well established. 6,117 HIV infection can  
involve either UMNs or LMNs, and patients predominantly pres-
ent with mixed UMN and LMN features. The disease may 
present at varying stages of HIV infection. A clinical pattern of 
progressive motor neuron degeneration over weeks rather than 
months along with pathologic features of vacuolar myelopathy 
and inflammation suggest an immune pathogenesis that is dis -
tinct from ALS. 166 Recognizing this syndrome is important be -
cause at least half of cases will respond to adequate antiretroviral 
therapy, further supporting a link to HIV infection and pathogen-
esis.6,148 Other infectious agents implicated in underlying MND, 
including Lyme disease (Borrelia burgdorferi) , herpes zoster, 
Creutzfeldt-Jacob disease, and human T-lymphotropic virus-1 
(HTLV-1), are more controversial. HTLV-1 infections are typi -
cally associated with an UMN-predominant syndrome of my -
elopathy called tropical spastic paraparesis or HTLV-1–associated 
myelopathy (HAM). 74,197 This disorder is distinct from MND 
because of the presence of symmetric lower limb involvement, 
sensory loss, and bowel and bladder changes. HAM occurs in 
about 1% to 3% of individuals infected with HTLV-1.197 HTLV-1 
infection is endemic in certain areas, and therefore serologic evi -
dence of prior infection should not be used to prove a causative 
relationship. Herpes zoster can result in segmental weakness and 
atrophy with features that overlap with MND. It is not usually 
considered to be an isolated disorder of the motor neuron but 
affects the root, plexus, nerve, brain, and rarely the spinal cord.
Electrical Injury
There are sparse reports of electrical injury preceding syndromes 
of motor neuron loss. 5 The clinical features of such disorders  
are associated with variable delays of days to years after electrical 
injury. The epicenter of motor neuron degeneration loss occurs  
at the site of the electrical entry or exit in 90% of cases. 5 Myelo-
pathic features can also be present in some cases, including  
sensory symptoms, pain, and bowel and bladder dysfunction.
Hereditary Bulbar Syndromes
Brown-Vialetto-Van Laere syndrome-1 (BVVLS-1) and Fazio-
Londe disease (FLD) are two closely related and extremely rare 
childhood syndromes caused by autosomal recessive mutations in 
the SLC52A1, SLC52A2, and SLC52A3 genes, which encode ribo-
flavin transport proteins.24 BVVLS-1 and FLD both cause progres-
sive motor neuron loss with prominent bulbar and respiratory  
involvement. Usually BVVLS-1 is distinguished from FLD by the 
universal presence of hearing loss, but the two syndromes are now 
usually considered synonymous. Onset of both syndromes typi -
cally occurs in the first 2 decades of life. The pathogenesis of both 
syndromes is related to riboflavin deficiency; treatment therefore 
includes high-dose riboflavin supplementation.
General Rehabilitation, Symptomatic  
and Medical Treatment of MND
Most MNDs have a profound impact on the overall life and func-
tion of affected patients, and a comprehensive program of reha -
bilitation with appropriate symptomatic and medical treatment is 
required to optimize patients’ function and quality of life. This is 
usually best achieved through a multidisciplinary approach, 
whether through a formal multidisciplinary clinic or the coordi -
nation of the various disciplines by the physiatrist treating the 
patient.
Exercise in MND
In general, exercise has been shown to be safe, effective, and well 
tolerated in MNDs. Yet, the role of exercise in managing MND 
is the subject of controversy, and research is lacking. Specifically 
in ALS, retrospective epidemiologic data have suggested increased 
MND, specifically ALS, attributable to increased participation in 
strenuous exercise. 98 Despite theoretic concerns of worsening 
weakness, no controlled outcome study has shown worsening  
after exercise in patients, and broadly, studies show benefits of 
moderate aerobic and resistance exercise in MND. 43,126,149 For 
ALS, which is a progressive disease, noncontrolled trials cannot 
demonstrate a greater decline in strength or function than what 
would naturally occur over time. A 2013 Cochrane review found 
limited evidence of benefit but no evidence of harm of exercise, 
and a 2020 meta-analysis concluded that long-term functional 
improvements but no change in quality of life or harm could be 
attributed to exercise. 126 Overall, at this point in time there are 
insufficient data to establish the benefit (or harm) of exercise in 
patients with ALS with a high degree of certainty, but functional 
benefits are likely, and the risk of harm is low.43
ROM and stretching are considered safe and should be pre -
scribed for all patients with ALS. This is especially helpful in 
preventing contractures that could be a source of pain later in the 
disease.49 Using the data available, it is reasonable to have patients 
or caregivers apply ROM and stretching exercises to all patients 
with ALS and to encourage more functional patients to engage in 
mild aerobic and resistance training. The latter can consist of mild 
to modest resistance exercises for 15 minutes twice daily. The 
potential for overwork weakness can be avoided by some com -
monsense precautions, such as avoiding resistance exercise to fa -
tigue or cramping, aerobic exercising to the point of significant 
dyspnea, eccentric exercises, or exercising muscles with significant 
weakness. An unusual amount of delayed-onset muscle soreness is 
a sign to decrease the intensity of the program going forward. 
Care must be taken to avoid falls and injuries. Stationary cycling 
or pool therapy can reduce this risk. Overall, while a definitive 
evidential basis is lacking, exercise remains one of the few mo -
dalities that can improve the function of a patient with ALS. 
Some form of exercise should be discussed with every patient with 
ALS. In addition to the benefits in function a reasonable exercise 
program can offer, it allows the patient to exert some control over 
the disease in an active manner.

SECTION 4  Issues in Specific Diagnoses
The effects of exercise in other MNDs are similarly hampered 
by a lack of data. A 2019 Cochrane review could draw no defini -
tive conclusions regarding the benefit or harm of exercise in 
SMA,17 nor could a 2021 Cochrane review of exercise in KD, 97 
though one patient with KD showed a subjective functional loss 
and increased CK after exercise. 159 European Federation of the 
Neurological Societies guidelines from 2006 indicate good evi -
dence for resistance and aerobic exercise in patients with PPS, 63 
but a Cochrane review including more recent trials could con -
clude only that there was very low-quality evidence that muscle 
strengthening of the thumb muscles leads to improved strength.108 
If practical, there is also Level B evidence that training in warm 
climates and nonswimming water exercises are particularly use -
ful.63 Overall, it is reasonable to prescribe exercise for patients 
with SMA, KD, and postpolio syndrome within the limitations 
discussed for ALS earlier.
Pain and Cramps in MND
Cramps are common in patients with various forms of MND. In 
KD, cramps are early features of the disease and are felt to be re -
lated to axonal hyperexcitability and may occur before symptoms 
of overt motor deficits. Cramps are similarly a common source of 
discomfort in patients with ALS and are common early in the 
disease. Mexiletine and ranolazine have been shown to reduced 
symptoms of muscle cramps. 35,120,150 Other potential treatments 
may include massage, physical therapy, magnesium, carbamaze -
pine, phenytoin, verapamil, and gabapentin, though none have 
been studied in ALS. 8 Pain is a common feature of ALS in later 
stages, seen in up to 75% of patients. 173 Contractures and pro -
longed immobilization may contribute to this. T reatment there -
fore should begin with ROM exercises. Physical modalities such 
as ice and heat can be used. If these are ineffective, acetaminophen 
or nonsteroidal antiinflammatory medications and antispasticity 
agents are commonly used. Opioids may be necessary in time. 
They are effective nearly 75% of the time and have the advantage 
of relieving air hunger and anxiety.146 Respiratory depression, loss 
of airway protection and cough, and constipation need to be 
monitored and dose-adjusted accordingly.
Bulbar Dysfunction
MNDs with significant bulbar involvement will cause issues with 
dysarthria and dysphagia and subsequent issues with communica-
tion and nutrition. Communication is a major factor in patients’ 
quality of life and can be impaired in any patient with MND and 
significant bulbar involvement. Early and frequent involvement 
of speech-language pathologists will help accommodate the result-
ing barriers to communication through exercises and specialized 
equipment. Dysphagia in MNDs with bulbar involvement will 
eventually impact nutrition, and management of nutrition is a 
major aspect of the overall treatment of patients with severe 
MNDs. When dysphagia is severe, the swallowing mechanism 
needs to be bypassed to achieve adequate nutrition. Monitoring 
of weight and other metrics becomes a necessary part of all follow-
up visits with severely affected patients.
Respiratory Insufficiency
Respiratory muscles may be affected by motor neuron loss similar 
to other muscles. This leads to respiratory insufficiency and, if 
respiratory function is not supported, eventual respiratory failure. 
Respiratory failure is the main source of mortality in patients with 
MNDs, and the choices and methods of compensating for respi -
ratory insufficiency ultimately can have a significant impact on 
the lives of patients. Patients who choose tracheostomy and long-
term ventilation will generally survive much longer but must 
consider the eventual possibility of a locked-in, noncommunica -
tive state.
Mood and Cognitive Disorders
Mood disorders are common in MNDs 185,198,201 and should be 
screened for and treated because they have a significant impact on 
the quality of life in patients with MND. T reatment recommen-
dations for patients with MND and mood disorders generally 
corresponds to the treatment of mood disorders in the general 
population, with some specific considerations for patients with 
ALS (see earlier).
Summary
MNDs represent a diverse group of disorders with the common 
feature of motor neuron loss and associated deficits within the 
motor system. The goal of this chapter is to provide the general 
framework to approach patients with suspected MND. The spec-
trum of disorders reviewed, while not all encompassing, includes 
the more common of these relatively uncommon disorders. The 
effects of MND are often devastating, and in most forms of 
MND, there are generally no cures. Nevertheless, there is a sur -
prising difference that an attentive and educated physician can 
make on extending and improving the lives of patients with 
MND, and there is real hope. At the same time as polio is being 
steadily eradicated, genetic treatments are now available for SMA, 
and potential treatments for ALS are hopefully on the horizon. In 
the meantime, a physiatrist’s view of the function of the entire 
patient, ability to coordinate with multiple disciplines, and focus 
on quality of life are ideal when treating a patient with MND. 
Proper diagnosis and management of MND have never been 
more important. A multidisciplinary approach is usually the most 
effective to address the complexities of diagnosis, prognosis, and 
management, and physiatrists have key roles in the detailed evalu-
ation, accurate and timely diagnosis, and treatment of patients 
with MND.
